([/seɪnt ˈluːɪs, sənt-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*saynt LOO-iss, sənt-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[11]](#cite_note-11) officially the **City of St. Louis**,[[12]](#cite_note-12) is an [independent city](/wiki/Independent_city_(United_States) "Independent city (United States)") on the eastern edge of [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), United States. It lies near the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and the [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") rivers. In 2020, the [city proper](/wiki/City_proper "City proper") had a population of 301,578,[[9]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-9) while [its metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis"), which extends into [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), had an estimated population of over 2.8 million. It is the [largest metropolitan area in Missouri](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_of_Missouri "List of metropolitan areas of Missouri") and the second-largest in Illinois. The city's [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") is the 20th-largest in the United States.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

**St. Louis** ([/seɪnt ˈluːɪs, sənt-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*saynt LOO-iss, sənt-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[11]](#cite_note-11) officially the **City of St. Louis**,[[12]](#cite_note-12) is an [independent city](/wiki/Independent_city_(United_States) "Independent city (United States)") on the eastern edge of [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), United States. It lies near the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and the [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") rivers. In 2020, the [city proper](/wiki/City_proper "City proper") had a population of 301,578,[[9]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-9) while [its metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis"), which extends into [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), had an estimated population of over 2.8 million. It is the [largest metropolitan area in Missouri](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_of_Missouri "List of metropolitan areas of Missouri") and the second-largest in Illinois. The city's [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") is the 20th-largest in the United States.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The land that became St. Louis had been occupied by [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") cultures for thousands of years before [European settlement](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas"). The city was founded on February 14, 1764, by French fur traders [Gilbert Antoine de St. Maxent](/wiki/Gilbert_Antoine_de_St._Maxent "Gilbert Antoine de St. Maxent"), [Pierre Laclède](/wiki/Pierre_Lacl%C3%A8de "Pierre Laclède"), and [Auguste Chouteau](/wiki/Auguste_Chouteau "Auguste Chouteau").[[14]](#cite_note-Cazorla_et_al-14) They named it for King [Louis IX of France](/wiki/Louis_IX_of_France "Louis IX of France"), and it quickly became the regional center of the French [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"). In 1804, the United States acquired St. Louis as part of the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"). In the 19th century, St. Louis developed as a major port on the Mississippi River; from 1870 until the 1920 census, it was the fourth-largest city in the country. It separated from [St. Louis County](/wiki/St._Louis_County,_Missouri "St. Louis County, Missouri") in 1877, becoming an [independent city](/wiki/Independent_city "Independent city") and limiting its political boundaries. In 1904, it hosted the [Louisiana Purchase Exposition](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition "Louisiana Purchase Exposition"), also known as the St. Louis World's Fair, and the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

St. Louis is designated as one of 173 [global cities](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The GDP of Greater St. Louis was $226.6 billion in 2023.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18) St. Louis has a diverse economy with strengths in the service, manufacturing, trade, transportation, and aviation industries.[[19]](#cite_note-19) It is home to sixteen [*Fortune* 1000](/wiki/Fortune_1000 "Fortune 1000") companies, six of which are also [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies.[[20]](#cite_note-:2-20) Federal agencies headquartered in the city or with significant operations there include the [Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_St._Louis "Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis"), the [U.S. Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture"), and the [National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency](/wiki/National_Geospatial-Intelligence_Agency "National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency").

The city's attractions include the 630-foot (192 m) [Gateway Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch") in [Downtown St. Louis](/wiki/Downtown_St._Louis "Downtown St. Louis"), the [St. Louis Zoo](/wiki/St._Louis_Zoo "St. Louis Zoo"), the [Missouri Botanical Garden](/wiki/Missouri_Botanical_Garden "Missouri Botanical Garden"), the [Saint Louis Art Museum](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Art_Museum "Saint Louis Art Museum"), and [Bellefontaine Cemetery](/wiki/Bellefontaine_Cemetery "Bellefontaine Cemetery").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Major research universities in Greater St. Louis include [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis"), [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)"), and the [University of Missouri–St. Louis](/wiki/University_of_Missouri%E2%80%93St._Louis "University of Missouri–St. Louis"). The [Washington University Medical Center](/wiki/Washington_University_Medical_Center "Washington University Medical Center") hosts an agglomeration of [medical and pharmaceutical institutions](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_St._Louis "List of hospitals in St. Louis"), including [Barnes-Jewish Hospital](/wiki/Barnes-Jewish_Hospital "Barnes-Jewish Hospital"). St. Louis has [four professional sports teams](/wiki/Sports_in_St._Louis "Sports in St. Louis"): the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"), the [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"), [St. Louis City SC](/wiki/St._Louis_City_SC "St. Louis City SC") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer"), and the [St. Louis BattleHawks](/wiki/St._Louis_BattleHawks "St. Louis BattleHawks") of the [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2024) "United Football League (2024)").

## History

Main article: [History of St. Louis](/wiki/History_of_St._Louis "History of St. Louis")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of St. Louis](/wiki/Timeline_of_St._Louis "Timeline of St. Louis").

### Mississippian culture and European exploration

Historical affiliations

> ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Royal_Standard_of_the_King_of_France.svg/40px-Royal_Standard_of_the_King_of_France.svg.png) [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") 1690s–1763  
> ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Flag_of_Spain_%281785%E2%80%931873%2C_1875%E2%80%931931%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Spain_%281785%E2%80%931873%2C_1875%E2%80%931931%29.svg.png) [Kingdom of Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") 1763–1800  
> ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Flag_of_France_official.svg/40px-Flag_of_France_official.svg.png) [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") 1800–1803  
> ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Flag_of_the_United_States.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_States.svg.png) [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") 1803–present

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Old_Chouteau_Mansion%2C_St._Louis._Mo_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Old_Chouteau_Mansion%2C_St._Louis._Mo_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Chouteau_Mansion,_St._Louis._Mo_(cropped).jpg)

The home of [Auguste Chouteau](/wiki/Auguste_Chouteau "Auguste Chouteau") is in St. Louis. [Gilbert Antoine de St. Maxent](/wiki/Gilbert_Antoine_de_St._Maxent "Gilbert Antoine de St. Maxent"),[[14]](#cite_note-Cazorla_et_al-14) Chouteau, and [Pierre Laclède](/wiki/Pierre_Lacl%C3%A8de "Pierre Laclède") founded St. Louis in 1764.

Main article: [History of St. Louis before 1762](/wiki/History_of_St._Louis_before_1762 "History of St. Louis before 1762")

The area that became St. Louis was a center of the [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture"), which built numerous temple and residential [earthwork](/wiki/Earthwork_(archaeology) "Earthwork (archaeology)") [mounds](/wiki/Mound_builder_(people) "Mound builder (people)") on both sides of the Mississippi River. Their major regional center was at [Cahokia Mounds](/wiki/Cahokia_Mounds "Cahokia Mounds"), active from 900 to 1500. Due to numerous major [earthworks](/wiki/Earthworks_(engineering) "Earthworks (engineering)") within St. Louis boundaries, the city was nicknamed "Mound City". These mounds were mostly demolished during the city's development. Historic Native American tribes in the area encountered by early Europeans included the [Siouan](/wiki/Siouan "Siouan")-speaking [Osage people](/wiki/Osage_people "Osage people"), whose territory extended west, and the [Illiniwek](/wiki/Illiniwek "Illiniwek").[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Sugarloaf Mound](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Mound "Sugarloaf Mound") in South St. Louis was repatriated to the [Osage Nation](/wiki/Osage_Nation "Osage Nation") in 2025.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

European exploration of the area was first recorded in 1673, when French explorers [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet") and [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") traveled through the Mississippi River valley. Five years later, [La Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_La_Salle "René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle") claimed the region for France as part of [La Louisiane](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)"), also known as [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"). The earliest European settlements in the [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country") (also known as Upper Louisiana) were built by the French during the 1690s and early 1700s at [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia,_Illinois "Cahokia, Illinois"), [Kaskaskia](/wiki/Kaskaskia,_Illinois "Kaskaskia, Illinois"), and [Fort de Chartres](/wiki/Fort_de_Chartres "Fort de Chartres"). Migrants from the French villages on the east side of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), such as Kaskaskia, also founded [Ste. Genevieve](/wiki/Ste._Genevieve,_Missouri "Ste. Genevieve, Missouri") in the 1730s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1764, after France lost the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), [Pierre Laclède](/wiki/Pierre_Lacl%C3%A8de "Pierre Laclède") and his stepson [Auguste Chouteau](/wiki/Auguste_Chouteau "Auguste Chouteau") founded what was to become the city of St. Louis.[[25]](#cite_note-25) (French lands east of the Mississippi had been ceded to [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and the lands west of the Mississippi to Spain; Catholic France and Spain were 18th-century allies. [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") of France and [Charles III of Spain](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain") were cousins, both from the House of Bourbon.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]) The French families built the city's economy on the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") with the Osage, and with more distant tribes along the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River"). The Chouteau brothers gained a monopoly from Spain on the fur trade with [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico"). French colonists used [African slaves](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_Missouri "History of slavery in Missouri") as domestic servants and workers in the city.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

During the negotiations for the 1763 [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), French negotiators agreed to transfer France's colonial territories west of the Mississippi and Missouri rivers to [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") to compensate for Spanish territorial losses during the war. These areas remained under Spanish control until 1803, when they were transferred to the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"). During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), St. Louis was unsuccessfully attacked by British-allied Native Americans in the 1780 [Battle of St. Louis](/wiki/Battle_of_St._Louis "Battle of St. Louis").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Founding

Main article: [History of St. Louis (1763–1803)](/wiki/History_of_St._Louis_(1763%E2%80%931803) "History of St. Louis (1763–1803)")

The founding of St. Louis was preceded by a trading business between [Gilbert Antoine de St. Maxent](/wiki/Gilbert_Antoine_de_St._Maxent "Gilbert Antoine de St. Maxent") and [Pierre Laclède (Liguest)](/wiki/Pierre_Lacl%C3%A8de "Pierre Laclède") in late 1763. St. Maxent invested in a Mississippi River expedition led by Laclède, who searched for a location to base the company's fur trading operations. Though [Ste. Genevieve](/wiki/Ste._Genevieve,_Missouri "Ste. Genevieve, Missouri") was already established as a trading center, he sought a place less prone to flooding. He found an elevated area overlooking the flood plain of the Mississippi River, not far south from its confluence with the Missouri and Illinois rivers. In addition to having an advantageous natural drainage system, there were nearby forested areas to supply timber and grasslands which could easily be converted for agricultural purposes. Laclède declared that this place "might become, hereafter, one of the finest cities in America". He dispatched his 14-year-old stepson, [Auguste Chouteau](/wiki/Auguste_Chouteau "Auguste Chouteau"), to the site, with the support of 30 settlers in February 1764.[[28]](#cite_note-wade3-28)

Laclède arrived at the future town site two months later and produced a plan for St. Louis based on the New Orleans street plan. The default block size was 240 by 300 feet, with just three long avenues running parallel to the west bank of the Mississippi. He established a public corridor of 300 feet fronting the river, but later this area was released for private development.[[28]](#cite_note-wade3-28)

[![This photograph of a mural titled Indian Attack on the Village of St. Louis, 1780, depicts the Battle of St. Louis.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/St-louis-attack.jpg/250px-St-louis-attack.jpg)](/wiki/File:St-louis-attack.jpg)

The mural *Indian Attack on the Village of St. Louis*, 1780, depicts that during the American Revolutionary War, St. Louis was unsuccessfully attacked by British-allied Native Americans in the [Battle of St. Louis](/wiki/Battle_of_St._Louis "Battle of St. Louis") in 1780.

For the city's first few years, it was not recognized by any governments. Although the settlement was thought to be under the control of the Spanish government, no one asserted any authority over it, and thus St. Louis had no local government. This vacuum led Laclède to assume civil control, and all problems were disposed in public settings, such as communal meetings. In addition, Laclède granted new settlers lots in town and the surrounding countryside. In hindsight, many of these original settlers thought of these first few years as "the golden age of St. Louis".[[29]](#cite_note-29) In 1763, the Native Americans in the region around St. Louis began expressing dissatisfaction with the victorious British, objecting to their refusal to continue to the French tradition of supplying gifts to Natives. Odawa chieftain [Pontiac](/wiki/Pontiac_(Odawa_leader) "Pontiac (Odawa leader)") began forming a pan-tribal alliance to counter British control over the region but received little support from the indigenous residents of St. Louis. By 1765, the city began receiving visits from representatives of the British, French, and Spanish governments.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

St. Louis was transferred to the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") in 1800 (although all of the colonial lands continued to be administered by Spanish officials), then sold by the French to the U.S. in 1803 as part of the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"). St. Louis became the capital of, and gateway to, the new territory. Shortly after [the official transfer of authority](/wiki/Three_Flags_Day "Three Flags Day") was made, the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") was commissioned by President Thomas Jefferson. The expedition departed from St. Louis in May 1804 along the Missouri River to explore the vast territory. There were hopes of finding a water route to the Pacific Ocean, but the party had to go overland in the Upper West. They reached the Pacific Ocean via the Columbia River in summer 1805. They returned, reaching St. Louis on September 23, 1806. Both Lewis and Clark lived in St. Louis after the expedition. Many other explorers, settlers, and trappers (such as [Ashley's Hundred](/wiki/Ashley%27s_Hundred "Ashley's Hundred")) would later take a similar route to the West.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 19th century

Main articles: [History of St. Louis (1804–1865)](/wiki/History_of_St._Louis_(1804%E2%80%931865) "History of St. Louis (1804–1865)") and [History of St. Louis (1866–1904)](/wiki/History_of_St._Louis_(1866%E2%80%931904) "History of St. Louis (1866–1904)")

See also: [St. Louis in the American Civil War](/wiki/St._Louis_in_the_American_Civil_War "St. Louis in the American Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/White_men_pose%2C_104_Locust_Street%2C_St._Louis%2C_Missouri_in_1852_at_Lynch%27s_Slave_Market_-_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-White_men_pose%2C_104_Locust_Street%2C_St._Louis%2C_Missouri_in_1852_at_Lynch%27s_Slave_Market_-_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:White_men_pose,_104_Locust_Street,_St._Louis,_Missouri_in_1852_at_Lynch%27s_Slave_Market_-_(cropped).jpg)

White men pose in 1852 at Lynch's [slave market](/wiki/Slave_market "Slave market") at 104 Locust Street

The city elected its first municipal legislators (called trustees) in 1808.[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Steamboats](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") first arrived in St. Louis in 1817, improving connections with [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") and eastern markets.[[31]](#cite_note-pimm1-31) Missouri was admitted as a state in 1821. St. Louis was incorporated as a city in 1822,[[32]](#cite_note-32) and continued to develop largely due to its busy [port](/wiki/Port "Port") and trade connections.[[33]](#cite_note-pimm2-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/City_of_Saint_Louis_and_Riverfront%2C_1874.jpg/250px-City_of_Saint_Louis_and_Riverfront%2C_1874.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_of_Saint_Louis_and_Riverfront,_1874.jpg)

City of St. Louis and Riverfront, 1874

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/St._Louis%2C_Mo._tornado_May_27%2C_1896_south_broadway.JPG/250px-St._Louis%2C_Mo._tornado_May_27%2C_1896_south_broadway.JPG)](/wiki/File:St._Louis,_Mo._tornado_May_27,_1896_south_broadway.JPG)

South Broadway had a tornado on May 27, 1896.

Immigrants from Ireland and Germany arrived in St. Louis in significant numbers starting in the 1840s, and the population of St. Louis grew from less than 20,000 inhabitants in 1840, to 77,860 in 1850, to more than 160,000 by 1860. By the mid-1800s, St. Louis had a greater population than New Orleans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Settled by many Southerners in a [slave state](/wiki/Free_and_slave_states "Free and slave states"), the city was split in political sympathies and became polarized during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). In 1861, 28 civilians were killed in a [clash with Union troops](/wiki/Camp_Jackson_Affair "Camp Jackson Affair"). The war hurt St. Louis economically, due to the [Union blockade](/wiki/Union_blockade "Union blockade") of river traffic to the south on the Mississippi River. The [St. Louis Arsenal](/wiki/St._Louis_Arsenal "St. Louis Arsenal") constructed [ironclads](/wiki/Ironclad "Ironclad") for the [Union Navy](/wiki/Union_Navy "Union Navy").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Slaves](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_Missouri "History of slavery in Missouri") worked in many jobs on the waterfront and on the riverboats. Given the city's location close to the [free state](/wiki/Free_and_slave_states "Free and slave states") of Illinois and others, some slaves escaped to freedom. Others, especially women with children, sued in court in [freedom suits](/wiki/Freedom_suits "Freedom suits"), and several prominent local attorneys aided slaves in these suits. About half the slaves achieved freedom in hundreds of suits before the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") began in 1861. The printing press of abolitionist [Elijah Parish Lovejoy](/wiki/Elijah_Parish_Lovejoy "Elijah Parish Lovejoy") was destroyed for the third time by townsfolk. He was murdered the next year in nearby [Alton, Illinois](/wiki/Alton,_Illinois "Alton, Illinois").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

After the war, St. Louis profited via trade with the West, aided by the 1874 completion of the [Eads Bridge](/wiki/Eads_Bridge "Eads Bridge"), named for its design engineer. Industrial developments on both banks of the river were linked by the bridge, the second in the Midwest over the Mississippi River after the [Hennepin Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Hennepin_Avenue_Bridge_(1855) "Hennepin Avenue Bridge (1855)") in Minneapolis. The bridge connects St. Louis, Missouri to [East St. Louis, Illinois](/wiki/East_St._Louis,_Illinois "East St. Louis, Illinois"). The Eads Bridge became a symbolic image of the city of St. Louis, from the time of its erection until 1965 when the [Gateway Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch") Bridge was constructed. The bridge crosses the St. Louis riverfront between Laclede's Landing, to the north, and the grounds of the Gateway Arch, to the south. Today the road deck has been restored, allowing vehicular and pedestrian traffic to cross the river. The St. Louis MetroLink light rail system has used the rail deck since 1993. An estimated 8,500 vehicles pass through it daily.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On August 22, 1876, the city of St. Louis voted to [secede](/wiki/Urban_secession "Urban secession") from [St. Louis County](/wiki/St._Louis_County,_Missouri "St. Louis County, Missouri") and become an independent city, and, following a recount of the votes in November, officially did so in March 1877.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The [1877 St. Louis general strike](/wiki/1877_St._Louis_general_strike "1877 St. Louis general strike") caused significant upheaval, in a fight for the eight-hour day and the banning of child labor.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Industrial production continued to increase during the late 19th century. Major corporations such as the [Anheuser-Busch](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch "Anheuser-Busch") brewery, [Ralston Purina](/wiki/Ralston_Purina "Ralston Purina") company and [Desloge Consolidated Lead Company](/wiki/Desloge_Consolidated_Lead_Company "Desloge Consolidated Lead Company") were established at St. Louis which was also home to several [brass era](/wiki/Brass_era "Brass era") automobile companies, including the [Success Automobile Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Success_Automobile_Manufacturing_Company "Success Automobile Manufacturing Company");[[37]](#cite_note-37) St. Louis is the site of the [Wainwright Building](/wiki/Wainwright_Building "Wainwright Building"), a skyscraper designed in 1892 by architect [Louis Sullivan](/wiki/Louis_Sullivan "Louis Sullivan").

### 20th century

Main article: [History of St. Louis (1905–1980)](/wiki/History_of_St._Louis_(1905%E2%80%931980) "History of St. Louis (1905–1980)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition_St._Louis_1904.jpg/250px-Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition_St._Louis_1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition_St._Louis_1904.jpg)

The Government Building is at the [1904 World's Fair](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition "Louisiana Purchase Exposition").

In 1900, the entire streetcar system was shut down by a [several months-long strike](/wiki/St._Louis_streetcar_strike_of_1900 "St. Louis streetcar strike of 1900"), with significant unrest occurring in the city & violence against the striking workers.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In 1904, the city hosted the [World's Fair](/wiki/1904_World%27s_Fair "1904 World's Fair") and the [Olympics](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics"), becoming the first non-European city to host the games.[[39]](#cite_note-1904_Olympics-39) The formal name for the 1904 World's Fair was the [Louisiana Purchase Exposition](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition "Louisiana Purchase Exposition"). Permanent facilities and structures remaining from the fair are located in [Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis) "Forest Park (St. Louis)"), and other notable structures within the park's boundaries include the [St. Louis Art Museum](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Art_Museum "Saint Louis Art Museum"), the [St. Louis Zoo](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Zoo "Saint Louis Zoo") and the [Missouri History Museum](/wiki/Missouri_History_Museum "Missouri History Museum"), and Tower Grove Park and the Botanical Gardens.

After the Civil War, social and racial discrimination in housing and employment were common in St. Louis. In 1916, during the [Jim Crow Era](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws"), St. Louis passed a residential segregation ordinance[[40]](#cite_note-40) saying that if 75% of the residents of a neighborhood were of a certain race, no one from a different race was allowed to move in.[[41]](#cite_note-41) That ordinance was struck down in a court challenge, by the NAACP,[[42]](#cite_note-42) after which racial covenants were used to prevent the sale of houses in certain neighborhoods to "persons not of Caucasian race".[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Again, St. Louisans offered a lawsuit in challenge, and such covenants were ruled unconstitutional by the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") in 1948 in *[Shelley v. Kraemer](/wiki/Shelley_v._Kraemer "Shelley v. Kraemer")*.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In 1926, [Douglass University](/wiki/Douglass_University "Douglass University"), a [historically black university](/wiki/Historically_black_university "Historically black university") was founded by [B. F. Bowles](/wiki/Benjamin_F._Bowles "Benjamin F. Bowles") in St. Louis, and at the time no other college in St. Louis County admitted black students.[[44]](#cite_note-:1-44)

In the first half of the 20th century, St. Louis was a destination in the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") of African Americans from the rural South seeking better opportunities.[[45]](#cite_note-45) During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [NAACP](/wiki/NAACP "NAACP") campaigned to integrate war factories. In 1964, [civil rights activists](/wiki/Civil_and_political_rights "Civil and political rights") protested at the construction of the Gateway Arch to publicize their effort to gain entry for African Americans into the skilled trade unions, where they were underrepresented. The Department of Justice filed the first suit against the unions under the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Between 1900 and 1929, St. Louis, had about 220 automakers, close to 10 percent of all American carmakers, about half of which built cars exclusively in St. Louis. Notable names include Dorris, Gardner and Moon.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In the first part of the century, St. Louis had some of the worst [air pollution in the United States](/wiki/Air_pollution_in_the_United_States "Air pollution in the United States"). In April 1940, the city banned the use of soft coal mined in nearby states. The city hired inspectors to ensure that only [anthracite](/wiki/Anthracite "Anthracite") was burned. By 1946, the city had reduced air pollution by about 75%.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/FromLacledesLanding.JPG/250px-FromLacledesLanding.JPG)](/wiki/File:FromLacledesLanding.JPG)

The [Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch") (completed 1965) is visible from [Laclede's Landing](/wiki/Laclede%27s_Landing "Laclede's Landing"), the remaining section of St. Louis's commercial riverfront.

*[De jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* educational segregation continued into the 1950s, and *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* segregation continued into the 1970s, leading to a court challenge and interdistrict desegregation agreement. Students have been bused mostly from the city to county school districts to have opportunities for integrated classes, although the city has created magnet schools to attract students.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

St. Louis, like many [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern "Midwestern") cities, expanded in the early 20th century due to industrialization, which provided jobs to new generations of immigrants and migrants from the South. It reached its peak population of 856,796 at the 1950 census.[[49]](#cite_note-heritage-49) [Suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization") from the 1950s through the 1990s dramatically reduced the city's population, as did restructuring of industry and loss of jobs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The effects of suburbanization were exacerbated by the small geographical size of St. Louis due to its earlier decision to become an independent city, and it lost much of its tax base. During the 19th and 20th century, most major cities aggressively annexed surrounding areas as residential development occurred away from the central city; however, St. Louis was unable to do so.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Several [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") projects were built in the 1950s, as the city worked to replace old and substandard housing. Some of these were poorly designed and resulted in problems. One prominent example, [Pruitt–Igoe](/wiki/Pruitt%E2%80%93Igoe "Pruitt–Igoe"), became a symbol of failure in public housing, and was torn down less than two decades after it was built.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The degradation and razing of [Mill Creek Valley](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Valley "Mill Creek Valley") in this time was featured as an example of disenfranchisement in the 2024 Reparations Commission Report.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Since the 1980s, several revitalization efforts have focused on [Downtown St. Louis](/wiki/Downtown_St._Louis "Downtown St. Louis").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 21st century

Main article: [History of St. Louis (1981–present)](/wiki/History_of_St._Louis_(1981%E2%80%93present) "History of St. Louis (1981–present)")

The urban revitalization projects that started in the 1980s continued into the new century. The city's [old garment district](/wiki/Washington_Avenue_Historic_District_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "Washington Avenue Historic District (St. Louis, Missouri)"), centered on Washington Avenue in the [Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_St._Louis "Downtown St. Louis") and [Downtown West](/wiki/Downtown_West,_St._Louis "Downtown West, St. Louis") neighborhoods, experienced major development starting in the late 1990s as many of the old factory and warehouse buildings were converted into lofts. The [American Planning Association](/wiki/American_Planning_Association "American Planning Association") designated Washington Avenue as one of 10 Great Streets for 2011.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The [Cortex Innovation Community](/wiki/Cortex_Innovation_Community "Cortex Innovation Community"), located within the city's [Central West End](/wiki/Central_West_End,_St._Louis "Central West End, St. Louis") neighborhood, was founded in 2002 and has become a multi-billion dollar economic engine for the region, with companies such as Microsoft and Boeing currently leasing office space.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) The [Forest Park Southeast](/wiki/Forest_Park_Southeast,_St._Louis "Forest Park Southeast, St. Louis") neighborhood in the central corridor has seen major investment starting in the early 2010s. Between 2013 and 2018, over $50 million worth of residential construction has been built in the neighborhood.[[54]](#cite_note-54) The population of the neighborhood has increased by 19% from the 2010 to 2020 Census.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") controversially returned to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in 2016. The city of St. Louis sued the NFL in 2017, alleging the league breached its own relocation guidelines to profit at the expense of the city. In 2021, the NFL and Rams owner [Stan Kroenke](/wiki/Stan_Kroenke "Stan Kroenke") agreed to settle out of court with the city for $790 million.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

On May 16, 2025, [a mile-wide EF3 tornado](/wiki/2025_St._Louis_tornado "2025 St. Louis tornado") struck northern parts of St. Louis, killing 5. According to mayor Cara Spencer, the tornado produced up to $1.6 billion in damage,[[58]](#cite_note-58) and governor Mike Kehoe stated on behalf of FEMA that the scope of residential damage was the worst since the [Joplin tornado](/wiki/Joplin_tornado "Joplin tornado") over a decade prior.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of St. Louis](/wiki/Geography_of_St._Louis "Geography of St. Louis")

### Topography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/62/St_Louis_Rivers.png)](/wiki/File:St_Louis_Rivers.png)

Rivers in the St. Louis area

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), St. Louis has a total area of 66 square miles (170 km2), of which 62 square miles (160 km2) is land and 4.1 square miles (11 km2) (6.2%) is water.[[60]](#cite_note-GR1-60) The city is built on [bluffs](/wiki/Cliff "Cliff") and terraces that rise 100–200 feet above the western banks of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), just south its confluence with the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") and directly across from [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"). The eastern city limits follow the Mississippi River as the state border.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Much of the area is a fertile and gently rolling prairie that features low hills and broad, shallow valleys. Both the Mississippi River and the Missouri River have cut large valleys with wide flood plains.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Near the southern boundary of the city of St. Louis (separating it from [St. Louis County](/wiki/St._Louis_County,_Missouri "St. Louis County, Missouri")) is the [River des Peres](/wiki/River_des_Peres "River des Peres"), practically the only river or stream within the city limits that is not entirely underground.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Most of River des Peres was confined to a channel or put underground in the 1920s and early 1930s. The lower section of the river was the site of some of the worst flooding of the [Great Flood of 1993](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1993 "Great Flood of 1993").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The St. Louis area experienced a significant flood in 1973, primarily caused by the Mississippi River reaching its highest level in over 150 years. While heavy rainfall and snowmelt contributed, the flood was exacerbated by a saturated drainage basin and high water levels in the Missouri River. The flooding led to levee failures, inundation of homes, businesses, and parkland, and prompted evacuations and sandbagging efforts.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

[Limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") and [dolomite](/wiki/Dolomite_(rock) "Dolomite (rock)") of the [Mississippian](/wiki/Mississippian_age "Mississippian age") [epoch](/wiki/Geologic_time_scale "Geologic time scale") underlie the area, and parts of the city are [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") in nature. This is particularly true of the area south of downtown, which has numerous sinkholes and caves. Most of the caves in the city have been sealed, but many springs are visible along the riverfront. Coal, brick [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), and [millerite](/wiki/Millerite "Millerite") ore were once mined in the city. The predominant surface rock, known as *St. Louis limestone*, is used as [dimension stone](/wiki/Dimension_stone "Dimension stone") and rubble for construction.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of St. Louis](/wiki/Architecture_of_St._Louis "Architecture of St. Louis")

See also: [List of tallest buildings in St. Louis](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_St._Louis "List of tallest buildings in St. Louis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Wainwright_Building%2C_7th_Street_and_Chestnut_Street%2C_St._Louis%2C_MO_-_53051647915.jpg/250px-Wainwright_Building%2C_7th_Street_and_Chestnut_Street%2C_St._Louis%2C_MO_-_53051647915.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wainwright_Building,_7th_Street_and_Chestnut_Street,_St._Louis,_MO_-_53051647915.jpg)

The [Wainwright Building](/wiki/Wainwright_Building "Wainwright Building") (1891) is an important [early skyscraper](/wiki/Early_skyscrapers "Early skyscrapers") designed by [Louis Sullivan](/wiki/Louis_Sullivan "Louis Sullivan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Lafayette_Square.jpg/250px-Lafayette_Square.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lafayette_Square.jpg)

Many houses in [Lafayette Square](/wiki/Lafayette_Square,_St._Louis "Lafayette Square, St. Louis") are built with a blending of Greek Revival, Federal and Italianate styles.

The architecture of St. Louis exhibits a variety of commercial, residential, and monumental [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture"). St. Louis is known for the [Gateway Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch"), the tallest [monument](/wiki/Monument "Monument") constructed in the United States at 630 feet (190 m).[[66]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost.com-66) The Arch pays homage to [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and St. Louis's position as the gateway to the West. Architectural influences reflected in the area include [French Colonial](/wiki/French_Colonial "French Colonial"), [German](/wiki/Architecture_of_Germany "Architecture of Germany"), [early American](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_United_States "Architecture of the United States"), and [modern architectural](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") styles.

Several examples of religious structures are extant from the pre-Civil War period, and most reflect the common residential styles of the time. Among the earliest is the [Basilica of St. Louis, King of France](/wiki/Basilica_of_St._Louis,_King_of_France "Basilica of St. Louis, King of France") (referred to as the *Old Cathedral*). The Basilica was built between 1831 and 1834 in the Federal style. Other religious buildings from the period include SS. Cyril and Methodius Church (1857) in the Romanesque Revival style and [Christ Church Cathedral](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "Christ Church Cathedral (St. Louis, Missouri)") (completed in 1867, designed in 1859) in the Gothic Revival style.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A few civic buildings were constructed during the early 19th century. The original St. Louis courthouse was built in 1826 and featured a Federal style stone facade with a rounded portico. However, this courthouse was replaced during renovation and expansion of the building in the 1850s. The [Old St. Louis County Courthouse](/wiki/Old_Courthouse_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "Old Courthouse (St. Louis, Missouri)") (known as the *Old Courthouse*) was completed in 1864 and was notable for having a [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") dome and for being the tallest structure in Missouri until 1894. Finally, a customs house was constructed in the Greek Revival style in 1852, but was demolished and replaced in 1873 by the [U.S. Customhouse and Post Office](/wiki/United_States_Customhouse_and_Post_Office_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "United States Customhouse and Post Office (St. Louis, Missouri)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Because much of the city's commercial and industrial development was centered along the riverfront, many pre-Civil War buildings were demolished during construction of the Gateway Arch. The city's remaining architectural heritage of the era includes a multi-block district of cobblestone streets and brick and cast-iron warehouses called [Laclede's Landing](/wiki/Laclede%27s_Landing "Laclede's Landing"). Now popular for its restaurants and nightclubs, the district is located north of Gateway Arch along the riverfront. Other industrial buildings from the era include some portions of the [Anheuser-Busch Brewery](/wiki/Anheuser%E2%80%93Busch#St._Louis_headquarters_and_brewery "Anheuser–Busch"), which date to the 1860s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

St. Louis saw a vast expansion in variety and number of religious buildings during the late 19th century and early 20th century. The largest and most ornate of these is the [Cathedral Basilica of St. Louis](/wiki/Cathedral_Basilica_of_St._Louis "Cathedral Basilica of St. Louis"), designed by [Thomas P. Barnett](/wiki/Thomas_P._Barnett "Thomas P. Barnett") and constructed between 1907 and 1914 in the [Neo-Byzantine](/wiki/Neo-Byzantine "Neo-Byzantine") style. The St. Louis Cathedral, as it is known, has one of the largest mosaic collections in the world. Another landmark in religious architecture of St. Louis is the [St. Stanislaus Kostka](/wiki/St._Stanislaus_Kostka_Church_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "St. Stanislaus Kostka Church (St. Louis, Missouri)"), which is an example of the [Polish Cathedral style](/wiki/Polish_Cathedral_style "Polish Cathedral style"). Among the other major designs of the period were [St. Alphonsus Liguori](/wiki/St._Alphonsus_Catholic_Church,_St._Louis "St. Alphonsus Catholic Church, St. Louis") (known as *The Rock Church*) (1867) in the Gothic Revival and [Second Presbyterian Church of St. Louis](/wiki/Second_Presbyterian_Church_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "Second Presbyterian Church (St. Louis, Missouri)") (1900) in [Richardsonian Romanesque](/wiki/Richardsonian_Romanesque "Richardsonian Romanesque").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By the [1900 census](/wiki/United_States_Census,_1900 "United States Census, 1900"), St. Louis was the fourth largest city in the country. In 1904, the city hosted a [world's fair](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair") at [Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "Forest Park (St. Louis, Missouri)") called the [Louisiana Purchase Exposition](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition "Louisiana Purchase Exposition"). Its architectural legacy is somewhat scattered. Among the fair-related cultural institutions in the park are the [St. Louis Art Museum](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Art_Museum "Saint Louis Art Museum") designed by [Cass Gilbert](/wiki/Cass_Gilbert "Cass Gilbert"), part of the remaining lagoon at the foot of Art Hill, and the Flight Cage at the [St. Louis Zoo](/wiki/St._Louis_Zoo "St. Louis Zoo"). The [Missouri History Museum](/wiki/Missouri_History_Museum "Missouri History Museum") was built afterward, with the profit from the fair. But 1904 left other assets to the city, like [Theodore Link](/wiki/Theodore_Link "Theodore Link")'s 1894 [St. Louis Union Station](/wiki/St._Louis_Union_Station "St. Louis Union Station"), and an improved Forest Park.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[One US Bank Plaza](/wiki/One_US_Bank_Plaza "One US Bank Plaza"), the local headquarters for [US Bancorp](/wiki/US_Bancorp "US Bancorp"), was constructed in 1976 in the [structural expressionist](/wiki/Structural_expressionist "Structural expressionist") style. Several notable [postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture "Postmodern architecture") commercial skyscrapers were built downtown in the 1970s and 1980s, including the [former AT&T building at 909 Chestnut Street](/wiki/909_Chestnut_Street "909 Chestnut Street") (1986), and [One Metropolitan Square](/wiki/One_Metropolitan_Square "One Metropolitan Square") (1989), which is the tallest building in St. Louis.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the 1990s, St. Louis saw the construction of the largest United States courthouse by area, the [Thomas F. Eagleton United States Courthouse](/wiki/Thomas_F._Eagleton_United_States_Courthouse "Thomas F. Eagleton United States Courthouse") (2000). The Eagleton Courthouse is home to the [United States District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Eastern_District_of_Missouri "United States District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri") and the [United States Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Eighth_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit"). The most recent high-rise buildings in St. Louis include two residential towers: [One Hundred](/wiki/One_Hundred_Above_the_Park "One Hundred Above the Park") in the Central West End neighborhood and [One Cardinal Way](/wiki/Ballpark_Village_(St._Louis) "Ballpark Village (St. Louis)") in the Downtown neighborhood.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Neighborhoods

Further information: [Neighborhoods of St. Louis](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_St._Louis "Neighborhoods of St. Louis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Blueberry_Hill_patio.jpg/250px-Blueberry_Hill_patio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blueberry_Hill_patio.jpg)

The [Delmar Loop](/wiki/Delmar_Loop "Delmar Loop") is a neighborhood close to [Washington University](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis"), bordering the city and [St. Louis County](/wiki/St._Louis_County,_Missouri "St. Louis County, Missouri").

The city is divided into 79 officially-recognized neighborhoods.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Climate

Further information: [Geography of St. Louis § Climate](/wiki/Geography_of_St._Louis#Climate "Geography of St. Louis")

The urban area of St. Louis has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfa*); however, its [metropolitan region](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis") even to the south may present a hot-summer [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfa*), which shows the effect of the [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") in the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The city experiences hot, humid summers and chilly to cold winters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is subject to both cold Arctic air and hot, humid tropical air from the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The average annual temperature recorded at nearby [Lambert–St. Louis International Airport](/wiki/Lambert%E2%80%93St._Louis_International_Airport "Lambert–St. Louis International Airport"), is 57.4 °F (14.1 °C). 100 and 0 °F (38 and −18 °C) temperatures can be seen on an average 3 and 1 days per year, respectively. Precipitation averages 41.70 inches (1,100 mm), but has ranged from 20.59 in (523 mm) in 1953 to 61.24 in (1,555 mm) in 2015. The highest recorded temperature in St. Louis was 115 °F (46 °C) on July 14, 1954, and the lowest was −22 °F (−30 °C) on January 5, 1884.

St. Louis experiences [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") 48 days a year on average.[[68]](#cite_note-weatherbase-68) Especially in the spring, these storms can often be severe, with high winds, large [hail](/wiki/Hail "Hail") and tornadoes. Lying within the hotbed of [Tornado Alley](/wiki/Tornado_Alley "Tornado Alley"), St. Louis is one of the most frequently tornado-struck metropolitan areas in the U.S. and has an extensive history of [damaging tornadoes](/wiki/St._Louis_tornado_history "St. Louis tornado history"). Severe flooding, such as the [Great Flood of 1993](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1993 "Great Flood of 1993"), may occur in spring and summer; the (often rapid) melting of thick snow cover upstream on the Missouri or Mississippi Rivers can contribute to springtime flooding.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Flora and fauna

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Tower_Grove_Park_Scene_1.jpg/250px-Tower_Grove_Park_Scene_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tower_Grove_Park_Scene_1.jpg)

[Tower Grove Park](/wiki/Tower_Grove_Park "Tower Grove Park") in spring

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Missouri_Botanical_Garden_-_Seiwa-en.JPG/250px-Missouri_Botanical_Garden_-_Seiwa-en.JPG)](/wiki/File:Missouri_Botanical_Garden_-_Seiwa-en.JPG)

[Missouri Botanical Garden](/wiki/Missouri_Botanical_Garden "Missouri Botanical Garden")

Before the founding of the city, the area was mostly prairie and open forest. Native Americans maintained this environment, good for hunting, by burning underbrush. Trees are mainly [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple"), and [hickory](/wiki/Hickory "Hickory"), similar to the forests of the nearby [Ozarks](/wiki/The_Ozarks "The Ozarks"); common understory trees include [eastern redbud](/wiki/Eastern_redbud "Eastern redbud"), [serviceberry](/wiki/Serviceberry "Serviceberry"), and [flowering dogwood](/wiki/Flowering_dogwood "Flowering dogwood"). [Riparian](/wiki/Riparian "Riparian") areas are forested with mainly [American sycamore](/wiki/American_sycamore "American sycamore").

Most of the residential areas of the city are planted with large native shade trees. The largest native forest area is found in Forest Park. In autumn, the changing color of the trees is notable. Most species here are typical of the eastern woodland, although numerous decorative non-native species are found. The most notable invasive species is [Japanese honeysuckle](/wiki/Japanese_honeysuckle "Japanese honeysuckle"), which officials are trying to manage because of its damage to native trees. It is removed from some parks.

Wildlife includes urbanized [coyotes](/wiki/Coyote "Coyote"), [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), [eastern gray squirrel](/wiki/Eastern_gray_squirrel "Eastern gray squirrel"), [cottontail rabbit](/wiki/Cottontail_rabbit "Cottontail rabbit"), and the nocturnal [Virginia opossum](/wiki/Virginia_opossum "Virginia opossum"). Large bird species are abundant in parks and include [Canada goose](/wiki/Canada_goose "Canada goose"), [mallard duck](/wiki/Mallard_duck "Mallard duck"), and [shorebirds](/wiki/Shorebird "Shorebird"), including the [great egret](/wiki/Great_egret "Great egret") and [great blue heron](/wiki/Great_blue_heron "Great blue heron"). [Gulls](/wiki/Gull "Gull") are common along the Mississippi River; these species follow [barge](/wiki/Barge "Barge") traffic.

Winter populations of [bald eagles](/wiki/Bald_eagle "Bald eagle") are along the Mississippi River around the [Chain of Rocks Bridge](/wiki/Chain_of_Rocks_Bridge "Chain of Rocks Bridge"). The city is on the [Mississippi Flyway](/wiki/Mississippi_Flyway "Mississippi Flyway"), used by migrating birds, and has a large variety of small bird species, common to the eastern U.S. The [Eurasian tree sparrow](/wiki/Eurasian_tree_sparrow "Eurasian tree sparrow"), an introduced species, is limited in North America to the counties surrounding St. Louis. The city has special sites for birdwatching of migratory species, including [Tower Grove Park](/wiki/Tower_Grove_Park "Tower Grove Park").

Common frog species include the [American toad](/wiki/American_toad "American toad") and species of chorus frogs called [spring peepers](/wiki/Spring_peeper "Spring peeper"), which are found in nearly every pond. Some years have outbreaks of [cicadas](/wiki/Cicada "Cicada") or [ladybugs](/wiki/Ladybug "Ladybug"). Mosquitoes, [no-see-ums](/wiki/No-see-ums "No-see-ums"), and houseflies are common insect nuisances, especially in July and August; because of this, windows are almost always fitted with screens. Invasive populations of [honeybees](/wiki/Western_honey_bee "Western honey bee") have declined in recent years. Numerous native species of pollinator insects have recovered to fill their ecological niche, and [armadillos](/wiki/Armadillos "Armadillos") are throughout the St. Louis area.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Pruitt-Igoe-overview.jpg/250px-Pruitt-Igoe-overview.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pruitt-Igoe-overview.jpg)

[Pruitt–Igoe](/wiki/Pruitt%E2%80%93Igoe "Pruitt–Igoe") was a large housing project constructed in 1954, which became infamous for poverty, crime and segregation. It was demolished in 1972.

St. Louis grew slowly until the American Civil War, when industrialization and immigration sparked a boom. Mid-19th century immigrants included many Irish and Germans; later there were immigrants from southern and eastern Europe. In the early 20th century, African American and white migrants came from the South; the former as part of the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") out of rural areas of the Deep South. Many came from Mississippi and Arkansas. Italians, Serbians, Lebanese, Syrians, and Greeks settled in St. Louis by the late 19th-Century.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

After years of immigration, migration, and expansion, the city reached its peak population in 1950. That year, the Census Bureau reported St. Louis's population as 82% [White](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States "Race and ethnicity in the United States") and 17.9% [African American](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States "Race and ethnicity in the United States").[[77]](#cite_note-census1-79) After World War II, St. Louis began losing population to the suburbs, first because of increased demand for new housing, unhappiness with city services, ease of commuting by highways, and later, [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight").[[78]](#cite_note-historical_populations-80) St. Louis's population decline has resulted in a significant increase of abandoned residential housing units and vacant lots throughout the city proper; this blight has attracted much wildlife (such as deer and coyotes) to the many abandoned overgrown lots.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As of the 2020 Census, St. Louis has lost 64.8% of its population since the [1950 United States census](/wiki/1950_United_States_census "1950 United States census"). During this period, the population of Greater St. Louis, which includes more than one county, has grown every year and continues to do so.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Ethnic_Origins_in_St._Louis%2C_MO.png/250px-Ethnic_Origins_in_St._Louis%2C_MO.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_Origins_in_St._Louis,_MO.png)

Ethnic origins in St. Louis

According to the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), St. Louis had 319,294 people living in 142,057 households, of which 67,488 households were families. The population density was 5,158.2 people per square mile (1,991.6 people/km2). About 24% of the population was 19 or younger, 9% were 20 to 24, 31% were 25 to 44, 25% were 45 to 64, and 11% were 65 or older. The median age was about 34 years.

The African-American population is concentrated in the north side of the city (the area north of Delmar Boulevard is 94.0% black, compared with 35.0% in the central corridor and 26.0% in the south side of St. Louis[[79]](#cite_note-81)). Among the Asian-American population in the city, the largest ethnic group is [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_American "Vietnamese American") (0.9%), followed by [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_American "Chinese American") (0.6%) and [Indians](/wiki/Indian_American "Indian American") (0.5%). The Vietnamese community has concentrated in the [Dutchtown](/wiki/Dutchtown,_St._Louis "Dutchtown, St. Louis") neighborhood of south St. Louis; Chinese are concentrated in the [Central West End](/wiki/Central_West_End,_St._Louis "Central West End, St. Louis").[[80]](#cite_note-82) People of [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American") descent are the largest Latino group, and make up 2.2% of St. Louis's population. They have the highest concentration in the Dutchtown, [Benton Park West](/wiki/Benton_Park_West,_St._Louis "Benton Park West, St. Louis") (Cherokee Street), and [Gravois Park](/wiki/Gravois_Park,_St._Louis "Gravois Park, St. Louis") neighborhoods.[[81]](#cite_note-censusus-83) People of [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") descent are concentrated in [The Hill](/wiki/The_Hill,_St._Louis "The Hill, St. Louis").

In 2010, St. Louis's per-capita rates of online charitable donations and volunteerism were among the highest among major U.S. cities.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=St._Louis&action=edit), 91.05% (270,934) of St. Louis city residents age 5 and older spoke English at home as a [primary language](/wiki/Primary_language "Primary language"), while 2.86% (8,516) spoke Spanish, 0.91% (2,713) Serbo-Croatian, 0.74% (2,200) Vietnamese, 0.50% (1,495) African languages, 0.50% (1,481) Chinese, and French was spoken as a [main language](/wiki/Main_language "Main language") by 0.45% (1,341) of the population over the age of five. In total, 8.95% (26,628) of St. Louis's population age 5 and older spoke a [mother language](/wiki/Mother_language "Mother language") other than English.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

### Bosnian population

See also: [History of the Bosnians in St. Louis](/wiki/History_of_the_Bosnians_in_St._Louis "History of the Bosnians in St. Louis")

About fifteen families from Bosnia settled in St. Louis between 1960 and 1970. After the [Bosnian War](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War") started in 1992, more Bosnian refugees began arriving and by 2000, tens of thousands of Bosnian refugees settled in St. Louis with the help of Catholic aid societies. Many of them were professionals and skilled workers who had to take any job opportunity to be able to support their families. Most Bosnian refugees are Muslim, ethnically [Bosniaks](/wiki/Bosniaks "Bosniaks") (87%); they have settled primarily in south St. Louis[[98]](#cite_note-100) and South County. [Bosnian-Americans](/wiki/Bosnian-Americans "Bosnian-Americans") are well integrated into the city, developing many businesses and ethnic/cultural organizations.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

An estimated 70,000 [Bosnians](/wiki/Bosnian_American "Bosnian American") live in the metro area, which is tied with [Chicago](/wiki/Bosnians_in_Chicago "Bosnians in Chicago") for largest population of Bosnians in the United States and the largest Bosnian population outside their homeland. The highest concentration of Bosnians is in the neighborhood of [Bevo Mill](/wiki/Bevo_Mill "Bevo Mill") and in [Affton](/wiki/Affton,_Missouri "Affton, Missouri"), [Mehlville](/wiki/Mehlville "Mehlville"), and [Oakville](/wiki/Oakville,_Missouri "Oakville, Missouri") of south [St. Louis County](/wiki/St._Louis_County,_Missouri "St. Louis County, Missouri").[[100]](#cite_note-102)[[101]](#cite_note-103)

Bosnian Muslim [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") have also settled in St. Louis.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

### Crime

Main article: [Crime in St. Louis](/wiki/Crime_in_St._Louis "Crime in St. Louis")

Since 2014 the city of St. Louis has had, as of April 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=St._Louis&action=edit), one of the highest murder rates, per capita, in the United States,[[103]](#cite_note-105) with 188 homicides in 2015 (59.3 homicides per 100,000)[[104]](#cite_note-crimerate-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107) and ranks No. 13 of the most dangerous cities in the world by homicide rate. [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Flint](/wiki/Flint,_Michigan "Flint, Michigan"), [Memphis](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee"), [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), and [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") have higher overall violent crime rates than St. Louis, when comparing other crimes such as rape, robbery, and aggravated assault.[[104]](#cite_note-crimerate-106)[[106]](#cite_note-108) These crime rates are high relative to other American cities, but St. Louis [index crime](/wiki/Index_crime "Index crime") rates have declined almost every year since the peak in 1993 (16,648), to the 2014 level of 7,931 (which is the sum of violent crimes and property crimes) per 100,000. In 2015, the index crime rate reversed the 2005–2014 decline to a level of 8,204. Between 2005 and 2014, violent crime has declined by 20%, although rates of violent crime remains 6 times higher than the [United States national average](/wiki/Crime_in_the_United_States "Crime in the United States") and property crime in the city remains 2 1⁄2 times the national average.[[107]](#cite_note-109) St. Louis has a higher homicide rate than the rest of the U.S. for both whites and blacks and a higher proportion committed by males. As of October 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=St._Louis&action=edit), 7 of the homicide suspects were white, 95 black, 0 Hispanic, 0 Asian and 1 female out of the 102 suspects. In 2016, St. Louis was the most dangerous city in the United States with populations of 100,000 or more, ranking 1st in violent crime and 2nd in property crime. It was also ranked 6th of the most dangerous of all establishments in the United States, and East St. Louis, a suburb of the city itself, was ranked 1st.[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111) The [St. Louis Police Department](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police_Department,_City_of_St._Louis "Metropolitan Police Department, City of St. Louis") at the end of 2016 reported a total of 188 murders for the year, the same number of homicides that had occurred in the city in 2015.[[110]](#cite_note-112) According to the STLP At the end of 2017, St. Louis had 205 murders but the city recorded only 159 inside St. Louis city limits.[[111]](#cite_note-2017murders-113)[[112]](#cite_note-114) The new Chief of Police, [John Hayden](/wiki/John_Hayden_Jr. "John Hayden Jr.") said two-thirds (67%) of all the murders and one-half of all the assaults are concentrated in a triangular area in the North part of the city.[[111]](#cite_note-2017murders-113)

Yet another factor when comparing the murder rates of St. Louis and other cities is the manner of drawing municipal boundaries. While many other municipalities have annexed many suburbs, St. Louis has not annexed as much suburban area as most American cities. According to a 2018 estimate, the St. Louis metro area included about 3 million residents and the city included about 300,000 residents. Therefore, the city contains about ten percent of the metro population, a low ratio indicating that the municipal boundaries include only a small part of the metro population.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of St. Louis](/wiki/Economy_of_St._Louis "Economy of St. Louis")

The [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of Greater St. Louis was $209.9 billion in 2022, up from $192.9 billion the previous year.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18) Greater St. Louis had a GDP per capita of $68,574 in 2021, up 10% from the previous year.[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-117) As of November, 2024, the education and health services industries employed the greatest number of people in the region, followed by the trade, transportation, and utilities industries and professional and business services.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

### Major companies and institutions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/StLouisABPackaging_Plant.JPG/250px-StLouisABPackaging_Plant.JPG)](/wiki/File:StLouisABPackaging_Plant.JPG)

The [Anheuser-Busch](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch "Anheuser-Busch") packaging plant is in St. Louis.

As of 2024, Greater St. Louis is home to six *Fortune* 500 companies: [Centene Corporation](/wiki/Centene_Corporation "Centene Corporation"), [Reinsurance Group of America](/wiki/Reinsurance_Group_of_America "Reinsurance Group of America"), [Emerson Electric](/wiki/Emerson_Electric "Emerson Electric"), [Edward Jones](/wiki/Edward_Jones_Investments "Edward Jones Investments"), [Graybar Electric](/wiki/Graybar "Graybar"), and [Ameren](/wiki/Ameren "Ameren"). An additional ten other area companies are listed on the *Fortune* 1000: [Post Holdings](/wiki/Post_Holdings "Post Holdings"), [Olin Corporation](/wiki/Olin_Corporation "Olin Corporation"), [Core & Main](/wiki/Core_%26_Main "Core & Main"), [Stifel Financial](/wiki/Stifel "Stifel"), [Peabody Energy](/wiki/Peabody_Energy "Peabody Energy"), [Arch Resources](/wiki/Arch_Resources "Arch Resources"), [Energizer Holdings](/wiki/Energizer "Energizer"), [Caleres](/wiki/Caleres "Caleres"), [Spire](/wiki/Spire_Inc. "Spire Inc."), and [Belden](/wiki/Belden_Incorporated "Belden Incorporated").[[20]](#cite_note-:2-20)

Other major corporations headquartered in the region include [Anheuser-Busch](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch "Anheuser-Busch"), [Bunge Global](/wiki/Bunge_Global "Bunge Global"), [Wells Fargo Advisors](/wiki/Wells_Fargo_Advisors "Wells Fargo Advisors"), [Enterprise Holdings](/wiki/Enterprise_Holdings "Enterprise Holdings"), [World Wide Technology](/wiki/World_Wide_Technology "World Wide Technology"), [Arco Construction](/w/index.php?title=Arco_Construction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arco Construction (page does not exist)"), [McCarthy Holdings](/w/index.php?title=McCarthy_Holdings&action=edit&redlink=1 "McCarthy Holdings (page does not exist)"), [Clayco Construction](/w/index.php?title=Clayco_Construction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clayco Construction (page does not exist)"), [Apex Oil](/w/index.php?title=Apex_Oil&action=edit&redlink=1 "Apex Oil (page does not exist)"), [Alberici](/w/index.php?title=Alberici_Corporation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alberici Corporation (page does not exist)"), and [Schnuck Market](/wiki/Schnucks "Schnucks").[[117]](#cite_note-119)[[118]](#cite_note-120) Notable corporations with operations in St. Louis but headquarters elsewhere include [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing"), [Bayer](/wiki/Bayer "Bayer"), [Mastercard](/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard"), [U.S. Bank](/wiki/U.S._Bancorp "U.S. Bancorp"), and [BMO Bank](/wiki/BMO_Harris_Bank "BMO Harris Bank").

The [Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_St._Louis "Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis") is one of two federal reserve banks in [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri").[[119]](#cite_note-121)

St. Louis is a center of medicine and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology").[[120]](#cite_note-122) The [Washington University School of Medicine](/wiki/Washington_University_School_of_Medicine "Washington University School of Medicine") is affiliated with [Barnes-Jewish Hospital](/wiki/Barnes-Jewish_Hospital "Barnes-Jewish Hospital"), the fifth largest hospital in the world. Both institutions operate the [Alvin J. Siteman Cancer Center](/wiki/Alvin_J._Siteman_Cancer_Center "Alvin J. Siteman Cancer Center").[[121]](#cite_note-123) The School of Medicine also is affiliated with [St. Louis Children's Hospital](/wiki/St._Louis_Children%27s_Hospital "St. Louis Children's Hospital"), one of the country's top pediatric hospitals.[[122]](#cite_note-124) Both hospitals are owned by [BJC HealthCare](/wiki/BJC_HealthCare "BJC HealthCare"). The [McDonnell Genome Institute](/wiki/McDonnell_Genome_Institute "McDonnell Genome Institute") at Washington University played a major role in the [Human Genome Project](/wiki/Human_Genome_Project "Human Genome Project").[[123]](#cite_note-125) [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)") Medical School is affiliated with [SSM Health](/wiki/SSM_Health "SSM Health")'s [Cardinal Glennon Children's Hospital](/wiki/Cardinal_Glennon_Children%27s_Hospital "Cardinal Glennon Children's Hospital") and [Saint Louis University Hospital](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_Hospital "Saint Louis University Hospital"). It also has a cancer center, vaccine research center, geriatric center, and a bioethics institute. Several different organizations operate hospitals in the area, including BJC HealthCare, [Mercy](/wiki/Mercy_(healthcare_organization) "Mercy (healthcare organization)"), SSM Health Care, and Tenet. Other health care and biotechnology institutions with operations in the region include [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer"), the [Donald Danforth Plant Science Center](/wiki/Donald_Danforth_Plant_Science_Center "Donald Danforth Plant Science Center"), Bayer, [Sigma-Aldrich](/wiki/Sigma-Aldrich "Sigma-Aldrich"), [Mallinckrodt](/wiki/Mallinckrodt "Mallinckrodt"), and [Multidata Systems International](/wiki/Multidata_Systems_International "Multidata Systems International").

Several once-independent pillars of the local economy have been purchased by other corporations. Among them are [Anheuser-Busch](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch "Anheuser-Busch"), purchased by Belgium-based [InBev](/wiki/InBev "InBev"); [Missouri Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Missouri_Pacific_Railroad "Missouri Pacific Railroad"), merged with the [Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska")-based [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") in 1982;[[124]](#cite_note-126) [McDonnell Douglas](/wiki/McDonnell_Douglas "McDonnell Douglas"), whose operations are now part of [Boeing Defense, Space & Security](/wiki/Boeing_Defense,_Space_%26_Security "Boeing Defense, Space & Security"); [Trans World Airlines](/wiki/Trans_World_Airlines "Trans World Airlines"), which was headquartered in the city for its last decade of existence prior to being acquired by [American Airlines](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines"); and [Ralston Purina](/wiki/Ralston_Purina "Ralston Purina"), now a wholly owned subsidiary of [Nestlé](/wiki/Nestl%C3%A9 "Nestlé").[[125]](#cite_note-127) The [May Department Stores Company](/wiki/May_Department_Stores_Company "May Department Stores Company") was purchased by [Federated Department Stores](/wiki/Federated_Department_Stores "Federated Department Stores"), now [Macy's](/wiki/Macy%27s "Macy's"), although it still has its regional headquarters in the area. Most of the assets of [Furniture Brands International](/wiki/Furniture_Brands_International "Furniture Brands International") were sold to [Heritage Home Group](/wiki/Heritage_Home_Group "Heritage Home Group") in 2013, which moved to [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina").[[126]](#cite_note-128)[[127]](#cite_note-129)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/JeffersonLake_BJC.jpg/250px-JeffersonLake_BJC.jpg)](/wiki/File:JeffersonLake_BJC.jpg)

[Barnes-Jewish Hospital](/wiki/Barnes-Jewish_Hospital "Barnes-Jewish Hospital") is affiliated with the [Washington University School of Medicine](/wiki/Washington_University_School_of_Medicine "Washington University School of Medicine").

[Cortex Innovation Community](/wiki/Cortex_Innovation_Community "Cortex Innovation Community") in [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_St._Louis "Midtown St. Louis") is the region's largest innovation hub. Cortex is home to offices of [Square](/wiki/Square,_Inc. "Square, Inc."), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), [Aon](/wiki/Aon_(company) "Aon (company)"), [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing"), and [Centene](/wiki/Centene "Centene"). Cortex has generated 3,800 tech jobs in 14 years, and once built out, is projected to generate $2 billion in development and create 13,000 jobs for the region.[[128]](#cite_note-brookings-stl-130) The nonprofit [Arch Grants](/wiki/Arch_Grants "Arch Grants") is attracting new startups to the region, while the nonprofit [LaunchCode](/wiki/LaunchCode "LaunchCode") trains future tech workers.[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)
