## Arts and culture

Main article: [Culture of St. Louis](/wiki/Culture_of_St._Louis "Culture of St. Louis")

See also: [Cuisine of St. Louis](/wiki/Cuisine_of_St._Louis "Cuisine of St. Louis"), [Landmarks of St. Louis](/wiki/Landmarks_of_St._Louis "Landmarks of St. Louis"), [List of museums in St. Louis](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_St._Louis "List of museums in St. Louis"), and [List of public art in St. Louis](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_St._Louis "List of public art in St. Louis")

| Name | Description | Photo |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Gateway Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch") | At 630 feet (190 m), the Gateway Arch is the world's tallest [arch](/wiki/Arch "Arch") and tallest human-made [monument](/wiki/Monument "Monument") in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").[[133]](#cite_note-135) Built as a monument to the [westward expansion of the United States](/wiki/Westward_expansion_of_the_United_States "Westward expansion of the United States"), it is the centerpiece of [Gateway Arch National Park](/wiki/Gateway_Arch_National_Park "Gateway Arch National Park") which was known as Jefferson National Expansion Memorial until 2018. |  |
| [St. Louis Art Museum](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Art_Museum "Saint Louis Art Museum") | Built for the [1904 World's Fair](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition "Louisiana Purchase Exposition"), with a building designed by [Cass Gilbert](/wiki/Cass_Gilbert "Cass Gilbert"), the museum houses paintings, sculptures, and cultural objects. The museum is located in [Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis) "Forest Park (St. Louis)"), and admission is free. |  |
| [Missouri Botanical Garden](/wiki/Missouri_Botanical_Garden "Missouri Botanical Garden") | Founded in 1859, the Missouri Botanical Garden is one of the oldest botanical institutions in the United States and a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"). It spans 79 acres in the [Shaw](/wiki/Shaw,_St._Louis "Shaw, St. Louis") neighborhood, including a 14-acre (5.7-hectare) [Japanese garden](/wiki/Japanese_garden "Japanese garden") and the Climatron [geodesic dome](/wiki/Geodesic_dome "Geodesic dome") conservatory. |  |
| [Cathedral Basilica of St. Louis](/wiki/Cathedral_Basilica_of_Saint_Louis_(St._Louis) "Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis (St. Louis)") | Dedicated in 1914, it is the mother church of the [Archdiocese of St. Louis](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_St._Louis "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of St. Louis") and the seat of its [archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop"). The church is known for its large [mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic "Mosaic") installation (which is one of the largest in the Western Hemisphere with 41.5 million pieces), burial crypts, and its outdoor sculpture. |  |
| [City Hall](/wiki/City_Hall_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "City Hall (St. Louis, Missouri)") | Located in [Downtown West](/wiki/Downtown_West,_St._Louis "Downtown West, St. Louis"), City Hall was designed by [Harvey Ellis](/wiki/Harvey_Ellis "Harvey Ellis") in 1892 in the [Renaissance Revival](/wiki/Renaissance_Revival_Architecture "Renaissance Revival Architecture") style. It is reminiscent of the [Hôtel de Ville, Paris](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Paris "Hôtel de Ville, Paris"). |  |
| [Central Library](/wiki/St._Louis_Public_Library "St. Louis Public Library") | Completed in 1912, the Central Library building was designed by [Cass Gilbert](/wiki/Cass_Gilbert "Cass Gilbert"). It serves as the main location for the [St. Louis Public Library](/wiki/St._Louis_Public_Library "St. Louis Public Library"). |  |
| [City Museum](/wiki/City_Museum_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "City Museum (St. Louis, Missouri)") | City Museum is a play house museum, consisting largely of repurposed architectural and industrial objects, housed in the former International Shoe building in the [Washington Avenue Loft District](/wiki/Washington_Avenue_Loft_District "Washington Avenue Loft District"). |  |
| [Old Courthouse](/wiki/Old_Courthouse_(St._Louis) "Old Courthouse (St. Louis)") | Built in the 19th century, it served as a federal and state courthouse. The [Scott v. Sandford](/wiki/Scott_v._Sandford "Scott v. Sandford") case (resulting in the Dred Scott decision) was tried at the courthouse in 1846. |  |
| [St. Louis Science Center](/wiki/St._Louis_Science_Center "St. Louis Science Center") | Founded in 1963, it includes a [science museum](/wiki/Science_museum "Science museum") and a [planetarium](/wiki/Planetarium "Planetarium"), and is situated in [Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis) "Forest Park (St. Louis)"). Admission is free. It is one of two science centers in the United States which offers free general admission. |  |
| [St. Louis Symphony](/wiki/St._Louis_Symphony "St. Louis Symphony") | Founded in 1880, the St. Louis Symphony Orchestra is the second oldest symphony orchestra in the United States, preceded by the [New York Philharmonic](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic "New York Philharmonic"). Its principal concert venue is [Powell Symphony Hall](/wiki/Powell_Symphony_Hall "Powell Symphony Hall"). |  |
| [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(St._Louis) "Union Station (St. Louis)") | Built in 1888, it was the city's main passenger intercity train terminal. Once the world's largest and busiest train station, it was converted in the 1980s into a hotel, [shopping center](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall"), and entertainment complex. Today, it also continues to serve local rail ([MetroLink](/wiki/MetroLink_(St._Louis) "MetroLink (St. Louis)")) transit passengers, with [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") service nearby. On December 25, 2019, the St. Louis Aquarium opened inside Union Station. The St. Louis Wheel, a 200 ft 42 gondola ferris wheel, is also located at Union Station. |  |
| [St. Louis Zoo](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Zoo "Saint Louis Zoo") | Built for the [1904 World's Fair](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase_Exposition "Louisiana Purchase Exposition"), it is recognized as a leading zoo in animal management, research, conservation, and education. It is located in [Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis) "Forest Park (St. Louis)"), and admission is free. |  |

The same year as the 1904 [World's Fair](/wiki/World%27s_Fair "World's Fair"), the Strassberger Music Conservatory Building was constructed at 2300 Grand. Otto Wilhelmi was the architect. In 1911, the conservatory had over 1,100 students.[[134]](#cite_note-136) The building is presently in the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[135]](#cite_note-137) A well known graduate was [Alfonso D'Artega](/wiki/Alfonso_D%27Artega "Alfonso D'Artega").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

With its French past and waves of Catholic immigrants in the 19th and 20th centuries, from Ireland, Germany and Italy, St. Louis is a major center of [Roman Catholicism in the United States](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_the_United_States "Roman Catholicism in the United States"). St. Louis also boasts the largest [Ethical Culture Society](/wiki/Ethical_Society "Ethical Society") in the United States and is one of the most generous cities in the United States, ranking ninth in 2013.[[136]](#cite_note-138) Several places of worship in the city are noteworthy, such as the [Cathedral Basilica of St. Louis](/wiki/Cathedral_Basilica_of_Saint_Louis "Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis"), home of the world's largest mosaic installation.[[137]](#cite_note-139) Other churches include the [Basilica of St. Louis, King of France](/wiki/Basilica_of_St._Louis,_King_of_France "Basilica of St. Louis, King of France"), the oldest Roman Catholic cathedral west of the Mississippi River and the oldest church in St. Louis; the [St. Louis Abbey](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Abbey "Saint Louis Abbey"), whose distinctive architectural style garnered multiple awards at the time of its completion in 1962; and [St. Francis de Sales Oratory](/wiki/St._Francis_de_Sales_Church_(St._Louis) "St. Francis de Sales Church (St. Louis)"), a [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") church completed in 1908 in South St. Louis and the second largest church in the city.

The city is identified with music and the performing arts, especially [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), and [ragtime](/wiki/Ragtime "Ragtime"). The [St. Louis Symphony](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Symphony "Saint Louis Symphony") is the second oldest [symphony orchestra](/wiki/Symphony_orchestra "Symphony orchestra") in the United States. Until 2010, it was also home to [KFUO-FM](/wiki/KFUO-FM "KFUO-FM"), one of the oldest classical music FM radio stations west of the Mississippi River.[[138]](#cite_note-140) [Opera Theatre of St. Louis](/wiki/Opera_Theatre_of_Saint_Louis "Opera Theatre of Saint Louis") has been called "one of America's best summer festivals" by the *Washington Post*. Former general director Timothy O'Leary was known for drawing the community into discussions of challenging operas. John Adams's "[The Death of Klinghoffer](/wiki/The_Death_of_Klinghoffer "The Death of Klinghoffer")", which touched off protests and controversy when performed by the [Metropolitan Opera](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera "Metropolitan Opera") in 2014, had no such problems in St. Louis three years before, because the company fostered a citywide discussion, with interfaith dialogues addressing the tough issues of terrorism, religion and the nature of evil that the opera brings up. St. Louis's Jewish Community Relations Council gave O'Leary an award. Under O'Leary, the company—always known for innovative work—gave second chances to other major American operas, such as John Corigliano's "[The Ghosts of Versailles](/wiki/The_Ghosts_of_Versailles "The Ghosts of Versailles")", presented in 2009 in a smaller-scale version.[[139]](#cite_note-141)

The [Gateway Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch") anchors downtown St. Louis and a historic center that includes: the Federal courthouse where the [Dred Scott case](/wiki/Dred_Scott_v._Sandford "Dred Scott v. Sandford") was first argued, an expanded public library, major churches and businesses, and retail.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] An increasing downtown residential population has taken to adapted office buildings and other historic structures. In nearby [University City](/wiki/University_City,_Missouri "University City, Missouri") is the [Delmar Loop](/wiki/Delmar_Loop "Delmar Loop"), ranked by the [American Planning Association](/wiki/American_Planning_Association "American Planning Association") as a "great American street" for its variety of shops and restaurants, and the Tivoli Theater, all within walking distance.[[140]](#cite_note-142)

Unique city and regional cuisine reflecting various immigrant groups include [toasted ravioli](/wiki/Toasted_ravioli "Toasted ravioli"), [gooey butter cake](/wiki/Gooey_butter_cake "Gooey butter cake"), [provel cheese](/wiki/Provel_cheese "Provel cheese"), the [slinger](/wiki/Slinger_(dish) "Slinger (dish)"), the [Gerber sandwich](/wiki/Gerber_sandwich "Gerber sandwich"), and the [St. Paul sandwich](/wiki/St._Paul_sandwich "St. Paul sandwich"). Some St. Louis chefs have begun emphasizing use of local produce, meats and fish, and neighborhood farmers' markets have become more popular. Artisan bakeries, salumeria, and chocolatiers also operate in the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[St. Louis-style pizza](/wiki/St._Louis-style_pizza "St. Louis-style pizza") has thin crust, provel cheese, and is cut in small squares.[[141]](#cite_note-143) Frozen-custard purveyor [Ted Drewes](/wiki/Ted_Drewes "Ted Drewes") offers its "Concrete": frozen custard blended with any combination of dozens of ingredients into a mixture so thick that a spoon inserted into the custard does not fall if the cup is inverted.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

## Sports

Main article: [Sports in St. Louis](/wiki/Sports_in_St._Louis "Sports in St. Louis")

See also: [Soccer in St. Louis](/wiki/Soccer_in_St._Louis "Soccer in St. Louis")

St. Louis hosts the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") and the [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"). In 2019, it became the eighth North American city to have won titles in all four major leagues (MLB, NBA, NFL, and NHL) when the Blues won the Stanley Cup championship. It has collegiate-level soccer teams and is one of three American cities to have hosted the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics"). A third major team, the [St. Louis City SC](/wiki/St._Louis_City_SC "St. Louis City SC") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer"), began play in 2023.

### Professional sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Busch_Stadium_2022.jpg/250px-Busch_Stadium_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Busch_Stadium_2022.jpg)

[Busch Stadium](/wiki/Busch_Stadium "Busch Stadium")

The [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") are one of the most successful franchises in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball").[[143]](#cite_note-145) The Cardinals have won 19 [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") (NL) titles (the [most pennants for the league franchise in one city](/wiki/List_of_National_League_pennant_winners "List of National League pennant winners")) and 11 [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") titles (second to the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") and the most by any NL franchise), recently in [2011](/wiki/2011_World_Series "2011 World Series").[[144]](#cite_note-146) They play at [Busch Stadium](/wiki/Busch_Stadium "Busch Stadium"). Previously, the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/History_of_the_St._Louis_Browns "History of the St. Louis Browns") played in the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) from 1902 to 1953, before moving to [Baltimore, Maryland](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") to become the current incarnation of the [Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles"). The [1944 World Series](/wiki/1944_World_Series "1944 World Series") was an all-St. Louis World Series, matching up the St. Louis Cardinals and St. Louis Browns at [Sportsman's Park](/wiki/Sportsman%27s_Park "Sportsman's Park"), won by the Cardinals in six games. It was the third and final time that the teams shared a home field. St. Louis also was home to the [St. Louis Stars](/wiki/St._Louis_Stars_(baseball) "St. Louis Stars (baseball)"), also known as the St. Louis Giants from 1906 to 1921, who played in [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") from 1920 to 1931 and won championships in 1928, 1930, and 1931, and the [St. Louis Maroons](/wiki/St._Louis_Maroons/Indianapolis_Hoosiers "St. Louis Maroons/Indianapolis Hoosiers") who played in the [Union Association](/wiki/Union_Association "Union Association") in 1884 and in the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") from 1885 to 1889. In 1884, The St. Louis Maroons won the Union Association pennant and started the season with 20 straight wins, a feat that was not surpassed by any major professional sports team in the United States until the [2015–16 Golden State Warriors season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Golden_State_Warriors_season "2015–16 Golden State Warriors season") when they started their NBA season with 24 straight wins.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/St._Louis_blues_home_enterprise_center.jpg/250px-St._Louis_blues_home_enterprise_center.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Louis_blues_home_enterprise_center.jpg)

[Enterprise Center](/wiki/Enterprise_Center "Enterprise Center")

The [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) play at the [Enterprise Center](/wiki/Enterprise_Center "Enterprise Center"). They were one of the six teams added to the NHL in the [1967 expansion](/wiki/1967_NHL_expansion "1967 NHL expansion"). The Blues went to the Stanley Cup finals in their first three years, but got swept every time. Although they were the first 1967 expansion team to make the Stanley Cup Finals, they were also the last of the 1967 expansion teams to win the Stanley Cup. They finally won their first [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") in [2019](/wiki/2019_Stanley_Cup_Finals "2019 Stanley Cup Finals") after beating the [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins") in the final. This championship made St. Louis the eighth city to win a championship in each of the four major U.S. sports. Prior to the Blues, the city was home to the [St. Louis Eagles](/wiki/St._Louis_Eagles "St. Louis Eagles"). The team played in the 1934–35 season.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

St. Louis has been home to four [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) teams. The [St. Louis All-Stars](/wiki/St._Louis_All-Stars "St. Louis All-Stars") played in the city in 1923, the [St. Louis Gunners](/wiki/St._Louis_Gunners "St. Louis Gunners") in 1934, the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/History_of_the_St._Louis_Cardinals_(NFL) "History of the St. Louis Cardinals (NFL)") from 1960 to 1987, and the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/History_of_the_St._Louis_Rams "History of the St. Louis Rams") from 1995 to 2015. The football [Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals") advanced to the [NFL playoffs](/wiki/National_Football_League_playoffs "National Football League playoffs") four times (1964, 1974, 1975 and 1982), never hosting in any appearance. They did, however, win the [1964 Playoff Bowl](/wiki/Playoff_Bowl "Playoff Bowl") for third place against the Green Bay Packers by a score of 24–17. The Cardinals moved to [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), in 1988. The [Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams") played at the [Edward Jones Dome](/wiki/The_Dome_at_America%27s_Center "The Dome at America's Center") from 1995 to 2015 and won [Super Bowl XXXIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIV "Super Bowl XXXIV") in 2000. They also went to [Super Bowl XXXVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVI "Super Bowl XXXVI") but lost to the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots"). The Rams then returned to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in 2016.[[145]](#cite_note-147)

The [St. Louis Hawks](/wiki/Atlanta_Hawks "Atlanta Hawks") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) played at [Kiel Auditorium](/wiki/Kiel_Auditorium "Kiel Auditorium") from 1955 to 1968. They won the [NBA championship](/wiki/List_of_NBA_champions "List of NBA champions") in [1958](/wiki/1958_NBA_Finals "1958 NBA Finals") and played in three other [NBA Finals](/wiki/NBA_Finals "NBA Finals"): [1957](/wiki/1957_NBA_Finals "1957 NBA Finals"), [1960](/wiki/1960_NBA_Finals "1960 NBA Finals"), and [1961](/wiki/1961_NBA_Finals "1961 NBA Finals"). In 1968 the Hawks moved to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"). St. Louis was also the home to the [St. Louis Bombers](/wiki/St._Louis_Bombers "St. Louis Bombers") of the [Basketball Association of America](/wiki/Basketball_Association_of_America "Basketball Association of America") from 1946 to 1949 and the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") from 1949 to 1950 and the [Spirits of St. Louis](/wiki/Spirits_of_St._Louis "Spirits of St. Louis") of the [American Basketball Association](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association "American Basketball Association") from 1974 to 1976 when the ABA and NBA merged.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![CityPark - St. Louis CITY SC](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/City_Park%2C_Saint_Louis.jpg/250px-City_Park%2C_Saint_Louis.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Park,_Saint_Louis.jpg)

[Energizer Park](/wiki/Energizer_Park "Energizer Park")

[Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer")'s [St. Louis City SC](/wiki/St._Louis_City_SC "St. Louis City SC") began play in 2023 at [Energizer Park](/wiki/Energizer_Park "Energizer Park"). Their [MLS Next Pro](/wiki/MLS_Next_Pro "MLS Next Pro") affiliate is [St. Louis City 2](/wiki/St._Louis_City_2 "St. Louis City 2"), which began play in 2022 and also plays at Energizer Park. Formerly, [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship")'s [Saint Louis FC](/wiki/Saint_Louis_FC "Saint Louis FC") played in the area from 2015 to 2020 at [World Wide Technology Soccer Park](/wiki/World_Wide_Technology_Soccer_Park "World Wide Technology Soccer Park").[[146]](#cite_note-148)

The [St. Louis BattleHawks](/wiki/St._Louis_BattleHawks "St. Louis BattleHawks") of the [XFL](/wiki/XFL_(2020) "XFL (2020)") began play in 2020, using [The Dome at America's Center](/wiki/The_Dome_at_America%27s_Center "The Dome at America's Center") as their home field. After a two-year hiatus of the league, the Battlehawks [returned in 2023](/wiki/2023_St._Louis_Battlehawks_season "2023 St. Louis Battlehawks season"), when the XFL resumed play.[[147]](#cite_note-149)

St. Louis hosts several minor league sports teams. The [Gateway Grizzlies](/wiki/Gateway_Grizzlies "Gateway Grizzlies") of the independent [Frontier League](/wiki/Frontier_League "Frontier League") play in the area in [Sauget, IL](/wiki/Grizzlies_Ballpark "Grizzlies Ballpark"). The [St. Louis Trotters](/wiki/St._Louis_Trotters "St. Louis Trotters") of the [Independent Basketball Association](/wiki/Independent_Basketball_Association "Independent Basketball Association") play at Matthews-Dickey Boys and Girls Club. The [St. Louis Ambush](/wiki/St._Louis_Ambush_(2013) "St. Louis Ambush (2013)") indoor soccer team plays in nearby [St. Charles](/wiki/St._Charles,_Missouri "St. Charles, Missouri") at the [Family Arena](/wiki/Family_Arena "Family Arena") as a part of the [Major Arena Soccer League](/wiki/Major_Arena_Soccer_League "Major Arena Soccer League"). The [St. Louis Slam](/wiki/St._Louis_Slam "St. Louis Slam") play in the [Women's Football Alliance](/wiki/Women%27s_Football_Alliance "Women's Football Alliance") at [Harlen C. Hunter Stadium](/wiki/Harlen_C._Hunter_Stadium "Harlen C. Hunter Stadium").

The region hosts [INDYCAR](/wiki/INDYCAR "INDYCAR"), [NHRA](/wiki/NHRA "NHRA") [drag racing](/wiki/Drag_racing "Drag racing"), and [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") events at [World Wide Technology Raceway at Gateway](/wiki/World_Wide_Technology_Raceway_at_Gateway "World Wide Technology Raceway at Gateway") in [Madison, Illinois](/wiki/Madison,_Illinois "Madison, Illinois"). [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") [flat racing](/wiki/Flat_racing "Flat racing") events are hosted at [Fairmount Park Racetrack](/wiki/Fairmount_Park_Racetrack "Fairmount Park Racetrack") near [Collinsville, Illinois](/wiki/Collinsville,_Illinois "Collinsville, Illinois").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Chess

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/SinquefieldCup2015.jpg/250px-SinquefieldCup2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:SinquefieldCup2015.jpg)

The [Sinquefield Cup](/wiki/Sinquefield_Cup "Sinquefield Cup") chess tournament is hosted annually in St. Louis.

St. Louis is home to the [Saint Louis Chess Club](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Chess_Club "Saint Louis Chess Club") where the [U.S. Chess Championship](/wiki/U.S._Chess_Championship "U.S. Chess Championship") is held. St. Louisan [Rex Sinquefield](/wiki/Rex_Sinquefield "Rex Sinquefield") founded the Chess Club and Scholastic Center of St. Louis (which was renamed as St. Louis Chess Club later) and moved the [World Chess Hall of Fame](/wiki/World_Chess_Hall_of_Fame "World Chess Hall of Fame") to St. Louis in 2011. The [Sinquefield Cup](/wiki/Sinquefield_Cup "Sinquefield Cup") Tournament started at St. Louis in 2013. In 2014 the Sinquefield Cup was the highest-rated chess tournament of all time. Former U.S. Chess Champions [Fabiano Caruana](/wiki/Fabiano_Caruana "Fabiano Caruana") and [Hikaru Nakamura](/wiki/Hikaru_Nakamura "Hikaru Nakamura") have lived in St. Louis. Former women's chess champion [Susan Polgar](/wiki/Susan_Polgar "Susan Polgar") also resides in St. Louis.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### College and amateur sports

St. Louis has hosted the [Final Four](/wiki/Final_Four "Final Four") of both the women's and men's [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") championship tournaments, and the men's [Frozen Four](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_ice_hockey_tournament "NCAA Division I men's ice hockey tournament") collegiate ice hockey tournament. [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)") has won 10 NCAA men's soccer championships, and the city has hosted the [College Cup](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Soccer_Championship "NCAA Division I Men's Soccer Championship") several times. In addition to collegiate soccer, many St. Louisans have played for the [United States men's national soccer team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team"), and 20 St. Louisans have been elected into the [National Soccer Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Soccer_Hall_of_Fame "National Soccer Hall of Fame"). St. Louis also is the origin of the sport of [corkball](/wiki/Corkball "Corkball"), a type of baseball in which there is no base running.[[148]](#cite_note-CorkballHistory-150)

Although the area does not have a [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") team, it hosts the St. Louis Phoenix, an [American Basketball Association](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association_(2000%E2%80%93present) "American Basketball Association (2000–present)") team.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Club Atletico Saint Louis](/wiki/Club_Atletico_Saint_Louis "Club Atletico Saint Louis"), a semi-professional soccer team, competes within the [National Premier Soccer League](/wiki/National_Premier_Soccer_League "National Premier Soccer League") and plays out of [St. Louis University High School Soccer Stadium](/wiki/St._Louis_University_High_School "St. Louis University High School").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Parks and recreation

Main article: [Parks in St. Louis](/wiki/Parks_in_St._Louis "Parks in St. Louis")

For parks in the region, see [Parks in Greater St. Louis](/wiki/Parks_in_Greater_St._Louis "Parks in Greater St. Louis").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Forest_Park%2C_St_Louis.jpg/250px-Forest_Park%2C_St_Louis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Forest_Park,_St_Louis.jpg)

[Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis) "Forest Park (St. Louis)") features a variety of attractions, including the [St. Louis Zoo](/wiki/St._Louis_Zoo "St. Louis Zoo"), the [St. Louis Art Museum](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Art_Museum "Saint Louis Art Museum"), the [Missouri History Museum](/wiki/Missouri_History_Museum "Missouri History Museum"), and the [St. Louis Science Center](/wiki/St._Louis_Science_Center "St. Louis Science Center").

The city operates more than 100 parks, with amenities that include sports facilities, playgrounds, concert areas, picnic areas, and lakes. [Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis) "Forest Park (St. Louis)"), located on the western edge of city, is the largest, occupying 1,400 acres of land, making it almost twice as large as [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") in New York City.[[66]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost.com-66) The park is home to five major institutions, including the [St. Louis Art Museum](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Art_Museum "Saint Louis Art Museum"), the [St. Louis Zoo](/wiki/St._Louis_Zoo "St. Louis Zoo"), the [St. Louis Science Center](/wiki/St._Louis_Science_Center "St. Louis Science Center"), the [Missouri History Museum](/wiki/Missouri_History_Museum "Missouri History Museum"), and [the Muny](/wiki/The_Muny "The Muny") amphitheatre.[[66]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost.com-66) Another significant park in the city is [Gateway Arch National Park](/wiki/Gateway_Arch_National_Park "Gateway Arch National Park"), which was known as Jefferson National Expansion Memorial until 2018 and is located on the riverfront in downtown St. Louis. The centerpiece of the park is the 630-foot (192 m) tall [Gateway Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch"), a [National Memorial](/wiki/National_Memorial_(United_States) "National Memorial (United States)") designed by noted architect [Eero Saarinen](/wiki/Eero_Saarinen "Eero Saarinen") and completed on October 28, 1965. Also part of the historic park is the [Old Courthouse](/wiki/Old_Courthouse_(St._Louis) "Old Courthouse (St. Louis)"), where the first two trials of *[Dred Scott v. Sandford](/wiki/Dred_Scott_v._Sandford "Dred Scott v. Sandford")* were held in 1847 and 1850.

Other parks include the [Missouri Botanical Garden](/wiki/Missouri_Botanical_Garden "Missouri Botanical Garden"), [Tower Grove Park](/wiki/Tower_Grove_Park "Tower Grove Park"), [Carondelet Park](/wiki/Carondelet_Park "Carondelet Park"), and [Citygarden](/wiki/Citygarden "Citygarden"). The Missouri Botanical Garden, a private garden and botanical research facility, is a National Historic Landmark and one of the oldest botanical gardens in the United States.[[66]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost.com-66) The Garden features 79 acres of horticultural displays from around the world. This includes a Japanese strolling garden, [Henry Shaw](/wiki/Henry_Shaw_(philanthropist) "Henry Shaw (philanthropist)")'s original 1850 estate home and a geodesic dome called the [Climatron](/wiki/Climatron "Climatron").[[66]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost.com-66) Immediately south of the Missouri Botanical Garden is [Tower Grove Park](/wiki/Tower_Grove_Park "Tower Grove Park"), a gift to the city by [Henry Shaw](/wiki/Henry_Shaw_(philanthropist) "Henry Shaw (philanthropist)"). Citygarden is an urban [sculpture park](/wiki/Sculpture_park "Sculpture park") located in downtown St. Louis, with art from [Fernand Léger](/wiki/Fernand_L%C3%A9ger "Fernand Léger"), [Aristide Maillol](/wiki/Aristide_Maillol "Aristide Maillol"), [Julian Opie](/wiki/Julian_Opie "Julian Opie"), [Tom Otterness](/wiki/Tom_Otterness "Tom Otterness"), [Niki de Saint Phalle](/wiki/Niki_de_Saint_Phalle "Niki de Saint Phalle"), and [Mark di Suvero](/wiki/Mark_di_Suvero "Mark di Suvero").[[149]](#cite_note-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152) The park is divided into three sections, each of which represent a different theme: river bluffs; flood plains; and urban gardens. Another downtown sculpture park is the Serra Sculpture Park, with the 1982 [Richard Serra](/wiki/Richard_Serra "Richard Serra") sculpture *Twain*.[[151]](#cite_note-serra-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/St._Louis%2C_Missouri_-_City_Hall_-_panoramio_%282%29.jpg/250px-St._Louis%2C_Missouri_-_City_Hall_-_panoramio_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Louis,_Missouri_-_City_Hall_-_panoramio_(2).jpg)

[City Hall](/wiki/City_Hall_(St._Louis) "City Hall (St. Louis)") was completed in 1898, evoking features of the [Hôtel de Ville, Paris](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Paris "Hôtel de Ville, Paris").

St. Louis is one of the 41 [independent cities in the U.S.](/wiki/Independent_city_(United_States) "Independent city (United States)") that does not legally belong to any [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)").[[154]](#cite_note-USDoC2001-156) St. Louis has a strong [mayor–council government](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government") with legislative authority and oversight vested in the [Board of Aldermen](/wiki/St._Louis_Board_of_Aldermen "St. Louis Board of Aldermen") and with executive authority in the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_St._Louis "Mayor of St. Louis") and six other elected officials.[[155]](#cite_note-citygovt-157) The mayor is the chief executive officer of the city and is responsible for appointing city department heads. The Board of Aldermen is made up of 14 members (one elected from each of the city's wards) plus a board president who is elected citywide.[[156]](#cite_note-158) The 2014 fiscal year budget topped $1 billion for the first time, a 1.9% increase over the $985.2 million budget in 2013.[[157]](#cite_note-2014budget-159) 238,253 registered voters lived in the city in 2012,[[158]](#cite_note-160) down from 239,247 in 2010, and 257,442 in 2008.[[159]](#cite_note-161)

Municipal elections in St. Louis are held in odd-numbered years, with the primary elections in March and the general election in April. The mayor is elected in odd-numbered years following the United States presidential election using a top-two [approval voting](/wiki/Approval_voting "Approval voting") primary.[[160]](#cite_note-162) The aldermen representing odd-numbered wards are up for election at the same time as the mayor. The president of the board of aldermen and the aldermen from even-numbered wards are elected in the off-years. The [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") has dominated St. Louis city politics for decades. The city has not had a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") mayor since 1949, and the last time a Republican was elected to another citywide office was in the 1970s. As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=St._Louis&action=edit), all 28 of the city's aldermen are Democrats.[[161]](#cite_note-163)

Forty-eight individuals have held the office of mayor of St. Louis, four of whom—[William Carr Lane](/wiki/William_Carr_Lane "William Carr Lane"), [John Fletcher Darby](/wiki/John_Fletcher_Darby "John Fletcher Darby"), [John Wimer](/wiki/John_Wimer "John Wimer"), and [John How](/wiki/John_How "John How")—served non-consecutive terms. The most terms served by a mayor was by Lane, who served 8 full terms plus the unexpired term of Darby. The current mayor is [Cara Spencer](/wiki/Cara_Spencer "Cara Spencer"), who took office on April 15, 2025. She is the city's third consecutive female mayor. Her predecessor [Tishaura Jones](/wiki/Tishaura_Jones "Tishaura Jones") who took office April 20, 2021, was the first African-American woman to hold the post. Jones succeeded [Lyda Krewson](/wiki/Lyda_Krewson "Lyda Krewson"), the first female mayor of the city, who retired in 2021 after serving for four years. The longest-serving mayor was [Francis Slay](/wiki/Francis_Slay "Francis Slay"), who took office April 17, 2001, and left office April 18, 2017, a total of 16 years and six days over four terms in office. The shortest-serving mayor was [Arthur Barret](/wiki/Arthur_Barret "Arthur Barret"), who died 11 days after taking office.

Although St. Louis separated from St. Louis County in 1876, some mechanisms have been put in place for joint funding management and funding of regional assets. The St. Louis Zoo-Museum district collects property taxes from residents of both St. Louis City and County, and the funds are used to support cultural institutions including the [St. Louis Zoo](/wiki/St._Louis_Zoo "St. Louis Zoo"), [St. Louis Art Museum](/wiki/Saint_Louis_Art_Museum "Saint Louis Art Museum") and the [Missouri Botanical Gardens](/wiki/Missouri_Botanical_Gardens "Missouri Botanical Gardens"). Similarly, the Metropolitan Sewer District provides sanitary and storm sewer service to the city and much of St. Louis County. The Bi-State Development Agency (now known as Metro) runs the region's [MetroLink](/wiki/MetroLink_(St._Louis) "MetroLink (St. Louis)") light rail system and bus system.

The City of St. Louis Sheriff's Office (STLSO or STLCSO) primarily provides security services for the courtrooms, and serves court documents and issues gun carry permits. In 2022, it gained the ability to make arrests and traffic stops.[[162]](#cite_note-164) Formed in 1876, the Sheriff's Office has over 200 employees, of which 165 are deputies. Its budget was $11.97 million in 2025.[[163]](#cite_note-StLgovt-165)

St. Louis is split between 8 districts in the [Missouri House of Representatives](/wiki/Missouri_House_of_Representatives "Missouri House of Representatives"): the 76th, 77th, 78th, 79th, 80th, 81st, 82nd, and 84th districts.[[165]](#cite_note-167) The 5th [Missouri Senate](/wiki/Missouri_Senate "Missouri Senate") district is entirely within the city, while the 4th is shared with St. Louis County.[[166]](#cite_note-168)

At the federal level, St. Louis is the heart of [Missouri's 1st congressional district](/wiki/Missouri%27s_1st_congressional_district "Missouri's 1st congressional district"), which also includes part of northern St. Louis County.[[167]](#cite_note-districtmaps-169) A Republican has not represented a significant portion of St. Louis in the U.S. House since 1953. The city shifted from Republican voting to a Democratic stronghold at the presidential level since 1928. [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in 1988 was the most recent Republican to win even a quarter of the city's votes in a presidential election.

The [United States Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Eighth_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit") and the [United States District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Eastern_District_of_Missouri "United States District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri") are based in the [Thomas F. Eagleton United States Courthouse](/wiki/Thomas_F._Eagleton_United_States_Courthouse "Thomas F. Eagleton United States Courthouse") in downtown St. Louis. St. Louis is also home to a [Federal Reserve System](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System "Federal Reserve System") branch, the [Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_St._Louis "Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis"). The [National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency](/wiki/National_Geospatial-Intelligence_Agency "National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency") (NGA) also maintains major facilities in the St. Louis area.[[168]](#cite_note-170)

## Education

Main article: [Education in St. Louis](/wiki/Education_in_St._Louis "Education in St. Louis")

For education in the region, see [Education in Greater St. Louis](/wiki/Education_in_Greater_St._Louis "Education in Greater St. Louis").

### Colleges and universities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/WUBrookings.JPG/250px-WUBrookings.JPG)](/wiki/File:WUBrookings.JPG)

[Brookings Hall](/wiki/Brookings_Hall "Brookings Hall") is at [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis").

The city is home to three national research universities, [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis"), [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)"), and the [University of Missouri-St. Louis](/wiki/University_of_Missouri-St._Louis "University of Missouri-St. Louis"), as classified under the [Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education"). [Washington University School of Medicine](/wiki/Washington_University_School_of_Medicine "Washington University School of Medicine") in St. Louis has been ranked among the top 10 medical schools in the country by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* for as long as the list has been published, and as high as second, in 2003 and 2004. *U.S. News & World Report* also ranks the undergraduate school and other graduate schools, such as the [Washington University School of Law](/wiki/Washington_University_School_of_Law "Washington University School of Law"), in the top 20 in the nation.[[66]](#cite_note-huffingtonpost.com-66)[[169]](#cite_note-171)

St. Louis Metropolitan Region is home to [St. Louis Community College](/wiki/St._Louis_Community_College "St. Louis Community College"). It is also home to several other four-year colleges & universities, including [Harris–Stowe State University](/wiki/Harris%E2%80%93Stowe_State_University "Harris–Stowe State University"), a [historically black](/wiki/Historically_black_colleges_and_universities "Historically black colleges and universities") [public university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university"), Webster University, Missouri Baptist University, University of Health Sciences & Pharmacy (the former Saint Louis College of Pharmacy), Southern Illinois University-Edwardsville (SIUE), and Lindenwood University.

In addition to Catholic theological institutions such as [Kenrick-Glennon Seminary](/wiki/Kenrick-Glennon_Seminary "Kenrick-Glennon Seminary") and [Aquinas Institute of Theology](/wiki/Aquinas_Institute_of_Theology "Aquinas Institute of Theology") sponsored by the [Order of Preachers](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"), St. Louis is home to three [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") seminaries: [Eden Theological Seminary](/wiki/Eden_Theological_Seminary "Eden Theological Seminary") of the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ"), [Covenant Theological Seminary](/wiki/Covenant_Theological_Seminary "Covenant Theological Seminary") of the [Presbyterian Church in America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_America "Presbyterian Church in America"), and [Concordia Seminary](/wiki/Concordia_Seminary "Concordia Seminary") of the St. Louis-based [Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church%E2%80%93Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod").

### Primary and secondary schools

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/SLUH.JPG/250px-SLUH.JPG)](/wiki/File:SLUH.JPG)

[St. Louis University High School](/wiki/St._Louis_University_High_School "St. Louis University High School") was founded in 1818. Its current building was built in 1924.

The [St. Louis Public Schools](/wiki/St._Louis_Public_Schools "St. Louis Public Schools") (SLPS), which covers the entire city,[[170]](#cite_note-172) operates more than 75 schools, attended by more than 25,000 students, including several [magnet schools](/wiki/Magnet_schools "Magnet schools"). Since 2017, SLPS operates under full [accreditation](/wiki/Accreditation "Accreditation") from the state of Missouri, having previously lost its accreditation in 2007.[[171]](#cite_note-173) The Board of Education of the city of St. Louis oversees the district.[[172]](#cite_note-174) Since 2000, [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools "Charter schools") have operated in the city of St. Louis using authorization from Missouri state law. These schools are sponsored by local institutions or corporations and take in students from [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") through high school.[[173]](#cite_note-175) In addition, several private schools exist in the city, and the [Archdiocese of St. Louis](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_St._Louis "Archdiocese of St. Louis") operates dozens of [parochial schools](/wiki/Parochial_schools "Parochial schools") in the city, including parochial high schools. The city also has several private high schools, including secular, [Montessori](/wiki/The_Fulton_School "The Fulton School"), [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school") and [Lutheran schools](/wiki/Lutheran_school "Lutheran school"). [St. Louis University High School](/wiki/St._Louis_University_High_School "St. Louis University High School") – a Jesuit preparatory high school founded in 1818 – is the oldest secondary educational institution in the U.S. west of the Mississippi River.[[174]](#cite_note-176) The state-operated K-12 boarding school [Missouri School for the Blind](/wiki/Missouri_School_for_the_Blind "Missouri School for the Blind") is in St. Louis.

## Media

Main article: [Media in St. Louis](/wiki/Media_in_St._Louis "Media in St. Louis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch_headquarters.JPG/250px-St._Louis_Post-Dispatch_headquarters.JPG)](/wiki/File:St._Louis_Post-Dispatch_headquarters.JPG)

The former *[St. Louis Post-Dispatch](/wiki/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch "St. Louis Post-Dispatch")* building is in downtown St. Louis.

[Greater St. Louis](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis") commands the 24th-largest [media market](/wiki/Media_market "Media market") in the United States.[[175]](#cite_note-177) All of the major U.S. television networks have affiliates in St. Louis, including [KTVI](/wiki/KTVI "KTVI") 2 ([Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")), [KMOV](/wiki/KMOV "KMOV") 4 ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), with [MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV") on channel 32.1), [KSDK](/wiki/KSDK "KSDK") 5 ([NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")), [KETC](/wiki/KETC "KETC") 9 ([PBS](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service")), [KPLR-TV](/wiki/KPLR-TV "KPLR-TV") 11 ([The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW")), [KNLC](/wiki/KNLC "KNLC") 24 ([MeTV](/wiki/MeTV "MeTV")), [KDNL](/wiki/KDNL "KDNL") 30 ([ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")), [WRBU](/wiki/WRBU "WRBU") 46 ([Ion](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television")), and [WPXS](/wiki/WPXS "WPXS") 51 [Daystar Television Network](/wiki/Daystar_Television_Network "Daystar Television Network"). Among the area's most popular radio stations are [KMOX](/wiki/KMOX "KMOX") (AM sports and talk, notable as the longtime flagship station for St. Louis Cardinals broadcasts), [KLOU](/wiki/KLOU "KLOU") (FM oldies), [WIL-FM](/wiki/WIL-FM "WIL-FM") (FM country), [WARH](/wiki/WARH "WARH") (FM adult hits), and [KSLZ](/wiki/KSLZ "KSLZ") (FM Top 40 mainstream).[[176]](#cite_note-arbitron-178) St. Louis also supports [public radio](/wiki/Public_radio "Public radio")'s [KWMU](/wiki/KWMU "KWMU"), an [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") affiliate. All-sports stations, such as [KFNS](/wiki/KFNS_(AM) "KFNS (AM)") 590 AM "The Fan" and [WXOS](/wiki/WXOS "WXOS") "101.1 ESPN" are also popular. [KSHE](/wiki/KSHE "KSHE") 95 FM "Real Rock Radio" has broadcast rock music since November 1967 - longer than any other radio station in the United States.

The *[St. Louis Post-Dispatch](/wiki/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch "St. Louis Post-Dispatch")* is the region's major newspaper. Others in the region include *[Ladue News](/w/index.php?title=Ladue_News&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ladue News (page does not exist)")*, *[West Newsmagazine](/w/index.php?title=West_Newsmagazine&action=edit&redlink=1 "West Newsmagazine (page does not exist)")*, the *[Webster-Kirkwood Times](/w/index.php?title=Webster-Kirkwood_Times&action=edit&redlink=1 "Webster-Kirkwood Times (page does not exist)"),* and the *[Call Newspapers](/w/index.php?title=Call_Newspapers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Call Newspapers (page does not exist)")* which all serve parts of St. Louis County. Three weeklies serve the African-American community: the *[St. Louis Argus](/wiki/St._Louis_Argus "St. Louis Argus")*, the *[St. Louis American](/wiki/St._Louis_American "St. Louis American")*, and the *[St. Louis Sentinel](/wiki/St._Louis_Sentinel "St. Louis Sentinel")*. *[St. Louis Magazine](/wiki/St._Louis_Magazine "St. Louis Magazine")*, a monthly magazine, covers topics such as local history, cuisine, and lifestyles, while the weekly *[St. Louis Business Journal](/wiki/St._Louis_Business_Journal "St. Louis Business Journal")* provides coverage of regional business news. St. Louis was served by an [online newspaper](/wiki/Online_newspaper "Online newspaper"), the *[St. Louis Beacon](/wiki/St._Louis_Beacon "St. Louis Beacon")*, but that publication merged with [KWMU](/wiki/KWMU "KWMU") in 2013.[[177]](#cite_note-beacon-179) The primary [alternative newspaper](/wiki/Alternative_newspaper "Alternative newspaper") was the *[Riverfront Times](/wiki/Riverfront_Times "Riverfront Times")* before it was closed in 2024.[[178]](#cite_note-180)

Many books and movies have been written about St. Louis. A few of the most influential and prominent films are *[Meet Me in St. Louis](/wiki/Meet_Me_in_St._Louis "Meet Me in St. Louis")* and *[American Flyers](/wiki/American_Flyers "American Flyers")*,[[179]](#cite_note-181) and novels include *[The Killing Dance](/wiki/The_Killing_Dance "The Killing Dance")*, *[Meet Me in St. Louis](/wiki/Meet_Me_in_St._Louis_(novel) "Meet Me in St. Louis (novel)")*, *[The Runaway Soul](/wiki/The_Runaway_Soul "The Runaway Soul")*, *[The Rose of Old St. Louis](/wiki/The_Rose_of_Old_St._Louis_(novel) "The Rose of Old St. Louis (novel)")*, and *[Circus of the Damned](/wiki/Circus_of_the_Damned "Circus of the Damned")*.

Because St. Louis was a prime location for immigrants to move to, much of the early social work depicting immigrant life was based on St. Louis, such as in the book *[The Immigrant in St. Louis](/wiki/The_Immigrant_in_St._Louis_(book) "The Immigrant in St. Louis (book)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Transportation

See also: [Transportation in Greater St. Louis](/wiki/Transportation_in_Greater_St._Louis "Transportation in Greater St. Louis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/St._Louis%2C_MO.jpg/250px-St._Louis%2C_MO.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Louis,_MO.jpg)

[Interstate 64](/wiki/Interstate_64 "Interstate 64") crosses the Mississippi in downtown St. Louis.

[Road](/wiki/Road_transport "Road transport"), [rail](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport"), [ship](/wiki/Ship_transport "Ship transport"), and [air](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation") transportation modes connect the city with surrounding communities in [Greater St. Louis](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis"), national transportation networks, and international locations. St. Louis also supports a [public transportation](/wiki/Public_transportation "Public transportation") network that includes [bus](/wiki/Public_transport_bus_service "Public transport bus service") and [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") service.[[180]](#cite_note-182)[[181]](#cite_note-183)

### Roads and highways

See also: [Streets of St. Louis](/wiki/Streets_of_St._Louis "Streets of St. Louis")

Four [interstate](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") highways connect the city to a larger regional highway system. [Interstate 70](/wiki/Interstate_70 "Interstate 70"), an east–west highway, runs from the northwest corner of the city to [downtown St. Louis](/wiki/Downtown_St._Louis "Downtown St. Louis"). The north–south [Interstate 55](/wiki/Interstate_55 "Interstate 55") enters the city at the south near the [Carondelet](/wiki/Carondelet,_Missouri "Carondelet, Missouri") neighborhood and runs toward the center of the city, and both [Interstate 64](/wiki/Interstate_64 "Interstate 64") and [Interstate 44](/wiki/Interstate_44 "Interstate 44") enter the city on the west, running parallel to the east. Two of the four interstates (Interstates 55 and 64) merge south of [Gateway Arch National Park](/wiki/Gateway_Arch_National_Park "Gateway Arch National Park") and leave the city on the [Poplar Street Bridge](/wiki/Poplar_Street_Bridge "Poplar Street Bridge") into Illinois, while Interstate 44 terminates at Interstate 70 at its new interchange near N Broadway and Cass Ave. A small portion of the [Interstate 270](/wiki/Interstate_270_(Missouri%E2%80%93Illinois) "Interstate 270 (Missouri–Illinois)") outer belt freeway runs through the northern end of the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 563-mile [Avenue of the Saints](/wiki/Avenue_of_the_Saints "Avenue of the Saints") links St. Louis with [Saint Paul, Minnesota](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Major roadways include the north–south [Memorial Drive](/wiki/Memorial_Drive_(St._Louis) "Memorial Drive (St. Louis)"), located on the western edge of Gateway Arch National Park and parallel to Interstate 70, the north–south streets of [Grand Boulevard](/wiki/Grand_Boulevard_(St._Louis) "Grand Boulevard (St. Louis)") and [Jefferson Avenue](/wiki/Jefferson_Avenue_(St._Louis) "Jefferson Avenue (St. Louis)"), both of which run the length of the city, and [Gravois Road](/wiki/Missouri_Route_30 "Missouri Route 30"), which runs from the southeastern portion of the city to downtown and used to be signed as [U.S. Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66"). An east-west roadway that connects the city with surrounding communities is [Martin Luther King, Jr. Drive](/wiki/Missouri_Route_180 "Missouri Route 180"), which carries traffic from the western edge of the city to downtown.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Buses and taxis

Main article: [MetroBus](/wiki/MetroBus_(St._Louis) "MetroBus (St. Louis)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/MetroBus_Route_97_to_Civic_Center_TC_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-MetroBus_Route_97_to_Civic_Center_TC_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MetroBus_Route_97_to_Civic_Center_TC_(cropped).jpg)

MetroBus in the [Delmar Loop](/wiki/Delmar_Loop "Delmar Loop") neighborhood bound for [Civic Center station](/wiki/Civic_Center_station_(MetroLink) "Civic Center station (MetroLink)")

Local bus service in the city of St. Louis is provided by the [Bi-State Development Agency](/wiki/Bi-State_Development_Agency "Bi-State Development Agency") via [MetroBus](/wiki/MetroBus_(St._Louis) "MetroBus (St. Louis)"), with more than 75 routes connecting to MetroLink light rail transit and stops in the city and region. The city is also served by [Madison County Transit](/wiki/Madison_County_Transit "Madison County Transit"), which connects downtown St. Louis to [Madison County, Illinois](/wiki/Madison_County,_Illinois "Madison County, Illinois"). National bus service in the city is offered by [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), [Burlington Trailways](/wiki/Burlington_Trailways "Burlington Trailways") and [Amtrak Thruway](/wiki/Amtrak_Thruway "Amtrak Thruway"), with a station at the [Gateway Transportation Center](/wiki/Gateway_Transportation_Center "Gateway Transportation Center"), and [Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(North_America) "Megabus (North America)"), with a stop at [St. Louis Union Station](/wiki/St._Louis_Union_Station "St. Louis Union Station").

[Taxicab](/wiki/Taxicab "Taxicab") service in the city is provided by private companies regulated by the Metropolitan Taxicab Commission. Rates vary by vehicle type, size, passengers and distance, and by regulation all taxicab fares must be calculated using a [taximeter](/wiki/Taximeter "Taximeter") and be payable in cash or credit card.[[182]](#cite_note-taxicode-184) Solicitation by a driver is prohibited, although a taxicab may be hailed on the street or at a stand.

### Light rail and subways

Main article: [MetroLink](/wiki/MetroLink_(St._Louis) "MetroLink (St. Louis)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/St._Louis_MetroLink_Blue_Line_Siemens_SD460_Cortex_Station_%2853685196808%29.jpg/250px-St._Louis_MetroLink_Blue_Line_Siemens_SD460_Cortex_Station_%2853685196808%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Louis_MetroLink_Blue_Line_Siemens_SD460_Cortex_Station_(53685196808).jpg)

A [St. Louis MetroLink](/wiki/St._Louis_MetroLink "St. Louis MetroLink") Blue Line train leaves [Cortex station](/wiki/Cortex_station "Cortex station")

The St. Louis metropolitan area is served by MetroLink (known as Metro) and is the 11th-largest light rail system in the country with 46 mi (74 km) of [double track](/wiki/Double_track "Double track") [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail"). The Red Line and The Blue Line both serve all the stations in the inner city, and branch to different destinations beyond in the suburban areas. Both lines enter the city north of [Forest Park](/wiki/Forest_Park_(St._Louis) "Forest Park (St. Louis)") on the western edge of the city or on the [Eads Bridge](/wiki/Eads_Bridge "Eads Bridge") in downtown St. Louis to Illinois. All of the system track is in independent right of way, with both surface level and underground subway track in the city. All stations are independent entry, and all platforms are flush-level with trains. Rail service is provided by the [Bi-State Development Agency](/wiki/Bi-State_Development_Agency "Bi-State Development Agency") (also known as Metro), which is funded by a [sales taxes](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") levied in the city and other counties in the region.[[183]](#cite_note-185) The [Gateway Multimodal Transportation Center](/wiki/Gateway_Multimodal_Transportation_Center "Gateway Multimodal Transportation Center") acts as the hub station in the city of St. Louis, linking the city's light rail system, local bus system, passenger rail service, and national bus service. It is located just east of the historic grand [St. Louis Union Station](/wiki/St._Louis_Union_Station "St. Louis Union Station").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Heavy rail

Main article: [Transportation in St. Louis § Railroad Service](/wiki/Transportation_in_St._Louis#Railroad_Service "Transportation in St. Louis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Terminal_Railroad_Association_of_St._Louis_Freight_Train.jpg/250px-Terminal_Railroad_Association_of_St._Louis_Freight_Train.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terminal_Railroad_Association_of_St._Louis_Freight_Train.jpg)

An eastbound [Terminal Railroad Association of St. Louis](/wiki/Terminal_Railroad_Association_of_St._Louis "Terminal Railroad Association of St. Louis") freight train passes under the Hampton Avenue viaduct

[Inter-city rail](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") passenger train service in the city is provided by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") at the [Gateway Multimodal Transportation Center](/wiki/Gateway_Multimodal_Transportation_Center "Gateway Multimodal Transportation Center") downtown. Amtrak trains terminating in the city include the *[Lincoln Service](/wiki/Lincoln_Service "Lincoln Service")* to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and the *[Missouri River Runner](/wiki/Missouri_River_Runner_(Amtrak) "Missouri River Runner (Amtrak)")* to [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"). St. Louis is an intermediate stop on the *[Texas Eagle](/wiki/Texas_Eagle "Texas Eagle")* route which provides long-distance passenger service between [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), San Antonio, and three days a week, to Los Angeles.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

St. Louis is the nation's third-largest freight rail hub, moving Missouri exports such as fertilizer, gravel, crushed stone, prepared foodstuffs, fats, oils, nonmetallic mineral products, grain, alcohol, tobacco products, automobiles, and automobile parts.[[185]](#cite_note-187) [Freight rail](/wiki/Freight_rail "Freight rail") service in St. Louis is provided on tracks owned by [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad"), [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway"), Foster Townsend Rail Logistics – formerly [Manufacturers Railway (St. Louis)](/wiki/Manufacturers_Railway_(St._Louis) "Manufacturers Railway (St. Louis)"), [Terminal Railroad Association of St. Louis](/wiki/Terminal_Railroad_Association_of_St._Louis "Terminal Railroad Association of St. Louis"), Affton Trucking,[[186]](#cite_note-afftontrucking.com-188) and the [BNSF Railway](/wiki/BNSF_Railway "BNSF Railway").

The [Terminal Railroad Association of St. Louis](/wiki/Terminal_Railroad_Association_of_St._Louis "Terminal Railroad Association of St. Louis") (reporting mark: TRRA) is a [switching and terminal railroad](/wiki/Switching_and_terminal_railroad "Switching and terminal railroad") jointly owned by all the major rail carriers in St. Louis. The company operates 30 [diesel-electric](/wiki/Diesel-electric "Diesel-electric") locomotives to move railcars around the [classification yards](/wiki/Classification_yards "Classification yards"), deliver railcars to local industries, and ready trains for departure.[[187]](#cite_note-189) The TRRA processes and dispatches a significant portion of railroad traffic moving through the city and owns and operates a network of rail bridges and tunnels including the [MacArthur Bridge (St. Louis)](/wiki/MacArthur_Bridge_(St._Louis) "MacArthur Bridge (St. Louis)") and the [Merchants Bridge](/wiki/Merchants_Bridge "Merchants Bridge").[[188]](#cite_note-190) This infrastructure is also used by [inter-city rail](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") and long-distance passenger trains serving St. Louis.

### Water

[River transportation](/wiki/River_transportation "River transportation") is available through the [Port of St. Louis](/wiki/Port_of_St._Louis "Port of St. Louis"), which is 19.3 miles of riverbank on the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") that handles more than 32 million tons of freight annually. The Port is the second largest inland port by trip-ton miles, and the third largest by tonnage in the United States, with more than 100 docks for [barges](/wiki/Barge "Barge") and 16 public terminals on the river.[[189]](#cite_note-191) The Port Authority added two new small fire and rescue craft in 2012 and 2013.

### Air

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Western_Perspective_of_St._Louis_Lambert_International_Airport_T1.jpg/250px-Western_Perspective_of_St._Louis_Lambert_International_Airport_T1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Western_Perspective_of_St._Louis_Lambert_International_Airport_T1.jpg)

[St. Louis Lambert International Airport](/wiki/St._Louis_Lambert_International_Airport "St. Louis Lambert International Airport") control tower and main terminal

St. Louis is served by two passenger airports. [St. Louis Lambert International Airport](/wiki/St._Louis_Lambert_International_Airport "St. Louis Lambert International Airport"), owned and operated by the City of St. Louis, is 11 miles northwest of downtown along highway I-70 between I-170 and I-270 in St. Louis County. It is the largest and busiest airport in the state. In 2016, when the airport had more than 255 daily departures to about 90 domestic and international locations, it served more than 15 million passengers.[[190]](#cite_note-flystl.com-192) The airport serves as a focus hub city for [Southwest Airlines](/wiki/Southwest_Airlines "Southwest Airlines"); it was once a hub for [Trans World Airlines](/wiki/Trans_World_Airlines "Trans World Airlines") and a focus-city for [American Airlines](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines") and [AmericanConnection](/wiki/AmericanConnection "AmericanConnection").[[190]](#cite_note-flystl.com-192) The airport has two terminals with a total of five concourses. International flights and passengers use Terminal 2, whose lower level holds the Immigration and Customs gates. Passengers can move between the terminals on complimentary buses that run continuously, or via MetroLink for a fee. It was possible to walk between the terminals until Concourse D was closed in 2008.[[191]](#cite_note-travelpulse.com-193)

[MidAmerica St. Louis Airport](/wiki/MidAmerica_St._Louis_Airport "MidAmerica St. Louis Airport") is the secondary passenger airport serving the metropolitan area. Located 17 miles east of the city downtown core, the airport serves domestic passengers. Air cargo transportation is available at Lambert International and at other nearby regional airports, including [MidAmerica St. Louis Airport](/wiki/MidAmerica_St._Louis_Airport "MidAmerica St. Louis Airport"), [Spirit of St. Louis Airport](/wiki/Spirit_of_St._Louis_Airport "Spirit of St. Louis Airport"), and [St. Louis Downtown Airport](/wiki/St._Louis_Downtown_Airport "St. Louis Downtown Airport").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
